The combination of AFC + balanced detection + lock-in demodulation enables detection of signals corresponding to as few as $10^{10}$ electron spins under favorable conditions. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern] #### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks **Discuss different types of sample cavities used in ESR spectrometers. Compare rectangular and cylindrical cavities in terms of mode, Q-factor, and sample access.** **Model Solution:** ESR sample cavities are hollow metallic resonators tuned to resonate at the klystron microwave frequency. They store microwave energy and concentrate the magnetic component of the microwave field $H_1$ at the sample, thereby enhancing the weak absorption signal. The two most common designs are: **1. Rectangular Cavity — TE₁₀₂ Mode:** - **Mode designation:** Transverse Electric, with 1 half-wave variation along the broad wall, 0 along the narrow wall, and 2 along the cavity length. - **Sample position:** The sample is inserted through a hole in the narrow wall, placed at the **$H_1$-field maximum** (electric field node) to maximize magnetic-dipole absorption and minimize dielectric losses. - **Inner dimensions (X-band):** Approximately 22.86 mm × 10.16 mm × 41.7 mm (WR-90 waveguide size, slightly modified). - **Quality Factor $Q$:** ≈ 3000–5000. - **Sample access:** Easy — a simple iris allows insertion of 4–5 mm OD quartz tubes. - **Construction:** Rectangular cross-section, often with one or both end walls removable for sample loading. - **Applications:** Routine X-band measurements; room-temperature work; simple aqueous samples. **2. Cylindrical Cavity — TE₀₁₁ Mode:** - **Mode designation:** Transverse Electric, with 0 azimuthal variation, 1 radial variation, and 1 axial variation. - **Sample position:** Sample inserted along the cylindrical axis through a hole in the end plate; positioned at the central $H_1$ maximum. - **Diameter:** ≈ 25 mm for X-band. - **Quality Factor $Q$:** ≈ 8000–10000 (much higher than rectangular). - **Sample access:** The cavity can accommodate a **quartz dewar** for variable-temperature work (liquid N₂ or He cooling), enabling measurements from 4 K to 300 K. - **Construction:** Cylindrical tube with movable end plunger for tuning; silver-plated interior for high conductivity. - **Applications:** High-sensitivity work, low-temperature studies, biological samples, aqueous solutions (using flat cells). **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Rectangular TE₁₀₂ | Cylindrical TE₀₁₁ | |---|---|---| | $Q$-factor | 3000–5000 | 8000–10000 | | Sensitivity | Moderate | High | | Sample size | 4–5 mm OD | Up to 10 mm OD | | Variable temperature | Limited | Excellent (Dewar fits) | | Tuning mechanism | Iris screw | Movable end plunger | | Field modulation | Easy (flat pole faces) | Easy | | Construction cost | Lower | Higher | **3. Other Specialized Cavities:** - **Loop-gap resonator:** A lumped-element resonator providing very high $H_1$ at low frequencies; used for lossy samples (aqueous biological specimens). - **Dielectric resonator:** Uses a high-permittivity ceramic puck; very compact and stable. - **Double (split) cavity:** Two resonant modes; one contains the sample and the other a reference standard (e.g., DPPH) for simultaneous calibration. --- #### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks **Explain the principle of field modulation and lock-in detection in ESR. Why is the output spectrum a first derivative?** **Model Solution:** **The Detection Problem:** A typical ESR absorption corresponds to a fractional power absorption of only $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ relative to the incident microwave power. Detecting such a small change on top of a large, fluctuating DC background (caused by klystron amplitude noise, source drift, and 1/f noise) is extremely difficult using DC detection alone. **Solution — Field Modulation:** A small sinusoidal modulation field $B_m \sin(\omega_m t)$ at frequency $f_m = 100$ kHz is superimposed on the main static field $B_0$ using a pair of Helmholtz coils mounted on the cavity pole pieces. The amplitude $B_m$ is chosen to be smaller than the ESR linewidth (typically $B_m \leq \Delta B_{pp}/5$) to avoid distortion. The total instantaneous field at the sample is: $$B(t) = B_0 + B_m \sin(\omega_m t)$$ The microwave absorption signal $A(B)$ is therefore modulated in time at $\omega_m$. Taylor-expanding around $B_0$: $$A(B_0 + B_m \sin\omega_m t) \approx A(B_0) + B_m \sin(\omega_m t) \left(\frac{dA}{dB}\right)_{B_0} + \frac{B_m^2}{4} \left(\frac{d^2A}{dB^2}\right)_{B_0} + \cdots$$ For small $B_m$, the leading time-varying term is at frequency $\omega_m$ with amplitude proportional to $dA/dB$. The DC term $A(B_0)$ is rejected; only the AC term carries information about the *slope* of the absorption lineshape. **Lock-in (Phase-Sensitive) Detection:** A **lock-in amplifier** multiplies the detector output by a reference signal at $\omega_m$ and integrates over many cycles. This: 1. Rejects all noise components not at $\omega_m$ (narrow-band detection). 2. Provides a DC output proportional to $dA/dB$. 3. Dramatically improves signal-to-noise ratio (often by factors of $10^3$–$10^4$). **Why the Output is a First Derivative:** The detected signal at the lock-in output is proportional to $dA/dB$. As $B_0$ is swept through the resonance: - At the **peak** of absorption ($dA/dB = 0$): output is zero. - On the **rising slope** ($dA/dB > 0$): output is positive. - On the **falling slope** ($dA/dB < 0$): output is negative. - At the **inflection points** of the absorption curve: output reaches its extrema. The resulting spectrum is a **first-derivative Lorentzian** (or Gaussian) curve, which crosses zero at the absorption maximum and has its peak-to-peak width equal to a specific fraction of the true linewidth (for Lorentzian, $\Delta B_{pp} \approx 0.577 \Delta B_{1/2}$). **Advantages of Derivative Presentation:** 1. **Baseline drift is invisible** — only the *change* in absorption is recorded. 2. **Resolution of overlapping lines** is enhanced because the derivative separates closely-spaced peaks more effectively. 3. **Linewidth measurement** is straightforward (peak-to-peak distance). 4. **Lock-in detection** rejects 1/f noise, improving SNR by orders of magnitude. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in ESR Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing g-factor for electron vs. nucleus** — Always use $g_e = 2.0023$ for *electron* spin. Nuclear g-factors ($g_N$) apply to NMR, not ESR. Mixing these is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > > 2. **Writing $\mu_B$ without specifying Bohr magneton** — Examiners look for **$\mu_B = 9.274 \times 10^{-24}$ J/T** explicitly in numerical problems. > > 3. **Forgetting units in the resonance condition** — $h\nu$ is in **Joules**, $g\mu_B B_0$ must also be in Joules. Mixing SI (T) with CGS (Gauss) for $\mu_B$ causes dimensional errors worth 2 marks. > > 4. **Drawing block diagrams without labels** — Each block must be named, and signal flow arrows must be shown. A diagram without directional arrows loses 2–3 marks. > > 5. **Stating "modulation is used to improve sensitivity" without explaining WHY** — You must mention the conversion to AC, lock-in detection, and rejection of 1/f noise to score full marks. > > 6. **Confusing TE₁₀₂ with TM₁₀₂** — ESR uses **TE modes** (transverse electric) because the magnetic component of the microwave must be aligned with the sample. TM modes would have a strong electric field at the sample, causing dielectric loss. > > 7. **Skipping the AFC loop** — When asked about the microwave bridge, the AFC reference cavity is mandatory; its absence costs 2 marks. > > 8. **Hyperfine line-counting errors** — For $n$ equivalent nuclei with spin $I$, the number of lines is $2nI + 1$, NOT $n + 1$ (the latter is only for $I = 1/2$). Mixing these up is a common trap. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist - [ ] **Resonance Condition:** $h\nu = g\mu_B B_0$ — the master equation of ESR. - [ ] **Free-electron g-factor:** $g_e = 2.002319$. - [ ] **Bohr magneton:** $\mu_B = 9.274 \times 10^{-24}$ J/T. - [ ] **X-band:** $\nu \approx 9.5$ GHz, $B_0 \approx 0.34$ T = **3400 G**. - [ ] **Q-band:** $\nu \approx 35$ GHz, $B_0 \approx 1.25$ T. - [ ] **Klystron** is the microwave source; reflex klystron uses repeller for frequency tuning. - [ ] **AFC loop** locks klystron frequency to a reference cavity for stability. - [ ] **Circulator/Isolator** ensures unidirectional microwave flow, protecting the source. - [ ] **Sample cavity** is a TE₁₀₂ (rectangular) or TE₀₁₁ (cylindrical) resonator with $Q \approx 3000$–$10000$. - [ ] **Sample placed at $H_1$-maximum / $E$-minimum** to satisfy magnetic-dipole selection rule. - [ ] **Field modulation** is at **100 kHz**, amplitude $B_m \leq \Delta B_{pp}/5$. - [ ] **Lock-in amplifier** converts modulated signal to DC; output is **first derivative** of absorption. - [ ] **Derivative spectrum** has zero crossing at resonance and peak-to-peak width $\Delta B_{pp}$. - [ ] **Hyperfine lines:** $N = 2nI + 1$ for $n$ equivalent nuclei of spin $I$. - [ ] **Spin Hamiltonian:** $\hat{H} = \mu_B \vec{B} \cdot \mathbf{g} \cdot \hat{S} + \hat{S} \cdot \mathbf{A} \cdot \hat{I} + \hat{S} \cdot \mathbf{D} \cdot \hat{S}$. - [ ] **ESR is selective** — only species with unpaired electrons give a signal. - [ ] **Boltzmann population ratio** at 300 K, X-band: $N_{\uparrow}/N_{\downarrow} \approx 1.0007$ — a tiny excess drives ESR. - [ ] **Gyromagnetic ratio of electron:** $\gamma_e \approx 660$ times that of proton → explains GHz frequencies. - [ ] **Cavity $Q$** determines sensitivity: higher $Q$ → stronger signal. - [ ] **1 T = 10⁴ Gauss** — unit conversion often tested. - [ ] **Common ESR standards:** DPPH ($g = 2.0036$), TEMPO ($g \approx 2.006$), Mn²⁺ marker. ### 🎯 Must-Know Mnemonics - **"GAME-DA"** for the spin Hamiltonian terms: **G**-tensor (Zeeman) → **A**-tensor (Hyperfine) → **M**ultiple spin → **E**nergy → **D**-tensor (ZFS) → **A** (Quadrupole). - **"KAVC-MAD"** for ESR block diagram: **K**lystron → **A**FC → **V**ariable attenuator → **C**avity → **M**odulation → **A**mplifier → **D**isplay. - **"X-Q-W"** for ESR bands: **X**-band (9.5 GHz), **Q**-band (35 GHz), **W**-band (95 GHz).

topics.title: Molecular Mass Spectrometry: Principle of Operation, Magnetic Deflection Mass Spectrometers, Components of a Mass Spectrometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT521 - ANALYTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Molecular Mass Spectrometry: Principle of Operation, Magnetic Deflection Mass Spectrometers, Components of a Mass Spectrometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Molecular Mass Spectrometry

**Mass Spectrometry (MS)** is an analytical technique that measures the **mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)** of ions, used to identify and quantify molecules based on their molecular mass and structural fragmentation patterns. It produces a **mass spectrum**, which is a plot of ion abundance (relative intensity) versus m/z.

In the context of **Molecular Mass Spectrometry** (a sub-discipline of MS), the technique is specifically employed to:
- Determine the **molecular weight** of organic and inorganic compounds.
- Elucidate **molecular structure** through fragmentation fingerprinting.
- Perform **isotopic analysis** of elements.
- Quantify trace analytes in complex matrices (e.g., environmental, biological, pharmaceutical samples).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *Mass Spectrometer* is NOT a spectrometer of electromagnetic radiation. It does not measure absorption, emission, or scattering of photons. Instead, it measures ions of matter in the gas phase under high vacuum. This is the most fundamental conceptual distinction students must grasp.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Molecular Sorting Machine"

Imagine a **postal sorting facility** in a large city:
1. **Letters (Ions)** arrive from different houses — each letter has a *mass* (weight) and a *postage stamp* (charge).
2. **Sorting Machine (Magnetic Sector)** uses a *curved conveyor belt* (magnetic field). Heavy letters fly off to the outer lanes; light letters curve tightly to the inner lanes. This separates them by **mass**.
3. **Counter (Detector)** at the end of each lane counts how many letters pass through.
4. The supervisor (**Data System**) tabulates the counts and produces a graph.

> **The Mass Spectrometer does exactly the same thing with molecules:** it ionizes them, sorts them by their mass-to-charge ratio using electric/magnetic fields, and detects how many of each ion arrive.

### 1.3 Why Mass Spectrometry? (Engineering Significance)

| Attribute | KTU-Level Definition | Practical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity** | Detection down to **femtomole (10⁻¹⁵ mol)** to **attomole (10⁻¹⁸ mol)** levels | Drug trace analysis, doping detection |
| **Specificity** | Unique m/z for each ion, with sub-ppm mass accuracy | Forensics, metabolomics |
| **Speed** | Modern instruments acquire **>100 spectra/second** | Real-time reaction monitoring (RGA) |
| **Versatility** | Couplable with **GC, LC, ICP** as inlet systems | Pharmaceutical, petrochemical, environmental |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Vacuum Metric:** Mass spectrometers operate under **High Vacuum (HV)** conditions, typically **10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁹ mbar**. This is essential to (a) prevent ion-molecule collisions that would scramble the mass spectrum, and (b) protect the filament/cathode from oxidation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principle of Operation — The 5 Sequential Stages

A mass spectrometer performs its analysis through **five sequential stages**, regardless of the specific instrument variant (magnetic sector, quadrupole, time-of-flight, etc.):

**Stage 1 — Sample Introduction (Inlet System)**
- The sample is introduced into the instrument via a suitable inlet — direct insertion probe, gas chromatograph, liquid chromatograph, or direct infusion.
- Vaporization occurs at temperatures appropriate to the analyte (room temperature for gases; up to 400°C for non-volatile organics).

**Stage 2 — Ionization**
- Neutral atoms/molecules are converted into ions (typically positive ions) by adding or removing electrons, protons, or other charged species.
- Common ionization methods: **Electron Impact (EI)**, **Chemical Ionization (CI)**, **Electrospray Ionization (ESI)**, **Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization (MALDI)**, **Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP)**.

**Stage 3 — Mass Analysis (Ion Separation)**
- Ions are separated according to their **m/z ratio** in a mass analyzer.
- The electric/magnetic field acts as a "mass filter," allowing only ions of a specific m/z to reach the detector at any given moment (scanning) or simultaneously (simultaneous detection).

**Stage 4 — Detection**
- The separated ions strike a detector, generating an electrical signal proportional to the number of ions.
- Common detectors: **Faraday Cup**, **Electron Multiplier (EM)**, **Microchannel Plate (MCP)**, **Photomultiplier (with dynode conversion)**.

**Stage 5 — Data Processing & Spectrum Generation**
- The instrument's data system converts the detector signal into a mass spectrum: a histogram/bar-graph plot of **relative abundance (%)** vs **m/z**.

### 2.2 The Physical Foundation — Charged Particle Dynamics

A magnetic deflection mass spectrometer separates ions based on the Lorentz force experienced by a moving charged particle in a magnetic field.

When a charged particle of charge **q** moves with velocity **v** perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field **B**, it experiences a force:

$$\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

Because the magnetic force is **always perpendicular to velocity**, it does no work on the ion. The ion's kinetic energy remains constant, and the magnetic field merely bends its trajectory into a **circular path** of radius **r**.

For an ion of mass **m** moving in a circular path, the centripetal force requirement gives:

$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

Rearranging for the radius of curvature:

$$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Core Insight:** The radius `r` is proportional to momentum `(mv)`, not mass alone. This is why mass spectrometers must first *accelerate* the ions to a known kinetic energy (or known velocity) before magnetic separation — otherwise, ions of the same mass but different velocities would arrive at different points on the detector.

### 2.3 The Acceleration Step — Linking KE to Velocity

Ions are accelerated through an electrostatic potential difference **V**. The work done by the electric field equals the kinetic energy gained:

$$qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Solving for velocity:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}}$$

Substituting this velocity expression into the radius equation:

$$r = \frac{m}{qB}\sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}} = \frac{1}{B}\sqrt{\frac{2mV}{q}}$$

Squaring both sides to obtain the **fundamental m/z equation of magnetic sector mass spectrometry**:

$$\boxed{\frac{m}{q} = \frac{B^2 r^2}{2V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since charge `q` for a singly-charged ion equals the elementary charge `e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C`, the equation is often written in terms of **m/z** where z is the charge number. For singly charged ions (z = 1), `m/z = m/e`.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r = \dfrac{mv}{qB}$ | Radius of circular motion of ion in B-field | Geometry of sector magnet design |
| 2 | $qV = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Kinetic energy acquired during acceleration | Determines ion velocity entering analyzer |
| 3 | $\dfrac{m}{q} = \dfrac{B^2 r^2}{2V}$ | **Master equation** of magnetic sector MS | Solves all m/z determination problems |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{m}{\Delta m} = \dfrac{r}{s_1 + s_2}$ | **Resolving power** of sector instrument | Determines ability to separate adjacent masses |
| 5 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2qV}{m}}$ | Velocity of ion after acceleration through V | Used in energy filter design |
| 6 | $f_c = \dfrac{qB}{2\pi m}$ | Cyclotron frequency for ion in B-field | Basis of FT-ICR mass spectrometers |
| 7 | $E_k = \dfrac{B^2 r^2 q}{2m}$ | Kinetic energy of ion on circular path | Electrostatic energy analyzer (ESA) equations |
| 8 | $s = \dfrac{r}{2}$ | Image width due to single slit of width r | Determines practical resolution limit |

Where:
- **m** = ion mass (kg)
- **q** = ion charge (C)
- **B** = magnetic flux density (Tesla)
- **r** = radius of curvature (m)
- **V** = accelerating voltage (V)
- **s₁, s₂** = slit widths (m)
- **R** = resolving power (dimensionless)
- **f_c** = cyclotron frequency (Hz)

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Consistency is Critical for KTU Marks:** Always convert atomic mass units (amu or u) to kilograms: `1 u = 1.6605 × 10⁻²⁷ kg`. The charge `q` must be in Coulombs, NOT in units of elementary charge `e`. Failure to use SI units is the **#1 cause of numerical errors** in KTU mass spectrometry problems.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The magnetic sector analyzer remains the **gold standard** in several industrial applications:
- **Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometry (IRMS)** in geochemistry and climate science.
- **Residual Gas Analysis (RGA)** in vacuum technology and semiconductor manufacturing.
- **Nuclear Safeguards** — uranium and plutonium isotope assays.
- **GD-MS (Glow Discharge Mass Spectrometry)** for direct solid analysis of high-purity metals and semiconductors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Derivation of the Master Mass Equation (m/q = B²r²/2V)

We begin with the **Lorentz Force equation** describing the force on a charged particle moving in a magnetic field:

$$\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

For the special case where the velocity is **perpendicular** to the magnetic field (the standard configuration in a magnetic sector), the magnitude of the force is:

$$F = qvB\sin(90°) = qvB$$

This force acts **perpendicular** to the velocity at every instant, producing **uniform circular motion**. In circular motion, this magnetic force provides the necessary **centripetal force**:

$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

This is **Equation 1** of the derivation.

**Step 1 — Solve for the radius of curvature**

Dividing both sides by `v`:

$$qB = \frac{mv}{r}$$

Solving for `r`:

$$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$

This is **Equation 2**.

**Step 2 — Relate velocity to the accelerating voltage**

The ion gains kinetic energy by falling through an electrostatic potential `V`:

$$qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Solving for `v`:

$$v^2 = \frac{2qV}{m}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}}$$

This is **Equation 3**.

**Step 3 — Substitute the velocity into the radius equation**

Plug Equation 3 into Equation 2:

$$r = \frac{m}{qB}\sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}}$$

Distribute the `m` inside the square root:

$$r = \frac{1}{qB}\sqrt{\frac{2m^2 qV}{m}}$$

$$r = \frac{1}{qB}\sqrt{2mqV}$$

**Step 4 — Square both sides**

$$r^2 = \frac{2mV}{qB^2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the mass-to-charge ratio**

Multiplying both sides by `B²`:

$$B^2 r^2 = \frac{2mV}{q}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\frac{m}{q} = \frac{B^2 r^2}{2V}}$$

**This is the master equation of magnetic deflection mass spectrometry**, and is the most frequently tested derivation in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** For a fixed sector magnet (constant `r`) at a fixed accelerating voltage `V`, the magnetic field `B` is the *only* variable that determines which m/z reaches the detector. This is why **scanning magnetic sector instruments** vary `B` over time to produce a mass spectrum.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem

**Problem:** A magnetic sector mass spectrometer has a sector radius of `r = 0.20 m` and is operated with an accelerating voltage of `V = 4000 V`. Calculate:
**(a)** The magnetic field strength `B` required to focus the ion `⁵⁶Fe⁺` (mass 56 u, charge +1 e).
**(b)** The magnetic field required to focus `²⁸Si⁺` (mass 28 u) under the same conditions.
**(c)** The radius at which `⁵⁶Fe⁺` would be focused if `B` were held at the value found in (b).

**Constants:**
- `1 u = 1.6605 × 10⁻²⁷ kg`
- `e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C`
- `r = 0.20 m`
- `V = 4000 V`

**Solution to Part (a):**

Convert the mass of ⁵⁶Fe⁺ to kg:

$$m_{Fe} = 56 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 9.299 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}$$

Using the master equation $\dfrac{m}{q} = \dfrac{B^2 r^2}{2V}$, solve for `B`:

$$B^2 = \frac{2V \cdot (m/q)}{r^2}$$

$$B^2 = \frac{2 \times 4000 \times (9.299 \times 10^{-26})/(1.602 \times 10^{-19})}{(0.20)^2}$$

Calculate the numerator carefully:

$$2 \times 4000 = 8000$$

$$\frac{9.299 \times 10^{-26}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 5.804 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/C}$$

$$8000 \times 5.804 \times 10^{-7} = 4.643 \times 10^{-3}$$

Calculate the denominator:

$$(0.20)^2 = 0.04$$

Therefore:

$$B^2 = \frac{4.643 \times 10^{-3}}{0.04} = 0.1161 \text{ T}^2$$

$$B = \sqrt{0.1161} = 0.341 \text{ T}$$

**B ≈ 0.341 Tesla** to focus ⁵⁶Fe⁺.

**Solution to Part (b):**

For ²⁸Si⁺:

$$m_{Si} = 28 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 4.649 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}$$

$$\frac{m}{q} = \frac{4.649 \times 10^{-26}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.902 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/C}$$

$$B^2 = \frac{2 \times 4000 \times 2.902 \times 10^{-7}}{0.04}$$

$$B^2 = \frac{2.322 \times 10^{-3}}{0.04} = 0.05804 \text{ T}^2$$

$$B = \sqrt{0.05804} = 0.241 \text{ T}$$

**B ≈ 0.241 Tesla** to focus ²⁸Si⁺.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Doubling the mass doubles the required B-field (at constant V and r). The relationship is `B ∝ √(m)`. This is the operational basis for mass scanning in a magnetic sector instrument.

**Solution to Part (c):**

If `B = 0.241 T` is held fixed and ⁵⁶Fe⁺ is accelerated through `V = 4000 V`, the new radius is:

$$r_{Fe} = \frac{1}{B}\sqrt{\frac{2m_{Fe} V}{q}}$$

$$r_{Fe} = \frac{1}{0.241}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.299 \times 10^{-26} \times 4000}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}}$$

Calculate the inside of the square root:

$$2 \times 9.299 \times 10^{-26} \times 4000 = 7.439 \times 10^{-22}$$

$$\frac{7.439 \times 10^{-22}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 4.643 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sqrt{4.643 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.06814$$

$$r_{Fe} = \frac{0.06814}{0.241} = 0.283 \text{ m}$$

**r_Fe ≈ 0.283 m** (which is longer than the 0.20 m sector, meaning the ion would *not* be focused at this detector position).

### 3.3 Derivation of Resolving Power (R = m/Δm)

The **resolving power** of a mass spectrometer is its ability to distinguish between two adjacent ion masses `m` and `m + Δm`. For a magnetic sector instrument with two defining slits of width `s₁` (source slit) and `s₂` (collector slit):

The image width at the detector is approximately equal to the sum of the two slit widths. The condition for two adjacent masses to be just resolved is:

$$\Delta x = s_1 + s_2$$

For a small mass difference `Δm`, the corresponding spatial separation at the detector is:

$$\Delta x = r \cdot \frac{\Delta m}{m}$$

This geometric relationship arises because the radial position of the ion's circular path is proportional to `√(m)`, and differentiating gives a linear relationship between displacement and small mass changes.

Setting these equal:

$$r \cdot \frac{\Delta m}{m} = s_1 + s_2$$

Solving for resolving power:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{m}{\Delta m} = \frac{r}{s_1 + s_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Insight:** To improve resolution, one can (a) increase the sector radius `r` (larger, more expensive magnet), or (b) decrease the slit widths `s₁` and `s₂` (at the cost of reduced sensitivity, since fewer ions pass through). The **Mattauch-Herzog geometry** is a special double-focusing design that achieves high resolution by combining a magnetic sector with an electrostatic energy analyzer.

### 3.4 Single Focusing vs. Double Focusing

**Single Focusing (Magnetic Sector Only):**
- Resolves ions by momentum `(mv)`, not by mass alone.
- Ions of the same m/z but slightly different kinetic energies (due to thermal spread at the source) arrive at slightly different radii → **peak broadening**.
- Resolving power: typically 1000–10,000.

**Double Focusing (Magnetic Sector + Electrostatic Analyzer):**
- Adds an **Electrostatic Analyzer (ESA)** before or after the magnetic sector.
- The ESA acts as an **energy filter** — only ions of a specific kinetic energy pass through.
- The magnetic sector then performs mass separation on these energy-purified ions.
- Resolving power: 10,000 to 100,000+ (sub-ppm mass accuracy).
- Used for exact mass determination in organic and isotope analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Mass Spectrometer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sampleIn["Sample Inlet System"] --> ionization["Ionization Source"]
    ionization --> accelerator["Ion Accelerator"]
    accelerator --> massAnalyzer["Mass Analyzer Sector"]
    massAnalyzer --> detector["Ion Detector"]
    detector --> dataSystem["Data Acquisition and Display"]
    vacuumPump["High Vacuum System 10 power -5 to 10 power -9 mbar"] -.-> sampleIn
    vacuumPump -.-> ionization
    vacuumPump -.-> massAnalyzer
    vacuumPump -.-> detector
    magnetCtrl["Magnetic Field Controller"] -.-> massAnalyzer
```

### 4.2 Detailed Functional Block Diagram — Magnetic Deflection Mass Spectrometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inletGroup["Sample Inlet Block"]
        direction TB
        inletA["Direct Insertion Probe"]
        inletB["GC or LC Coupling"]
        inletC["Batch Inlet for Gases"]
    end

    subgraph sourceGroup["Ion Source Block"]
        direction TB
        sourceA["Filament Cathode"]
        sourceB["Electron Impact EI Region"]
        sourceC["Ion Repeller and Extraction Plates"]
    end

    subgraph accelGroup["Acceleration Region"]
        direction TB
        accelA["Slit S1"]
        accelB["High Voltage Potential V"]
    end

    subgraph analysisGroup["Magnetic Sector Analyzer Block"]
        direction TB
        sectorA["Sector Magnet with Curved Pole Pieces"]
        sectorB["Radius of Curvature r equal to 0.1 to 0.5 m"]
        sectorC["Variable Magnetic Field B"]
    end

    subgraph detectionGroup["Detection Block"]
        direction TB
        detectA["Collector Slit S2"]
        detectB["Faraday Cup or Electron Multiplier"]
        detectC["Signal Amplifier"]
    end

    subgraph supportGroup["Support Systems"]
        direction TB
        supportA["Vacuum Pumps Turbomolecular and Rotary"]
        supportB["Magnet Power Supply"]
        supportC["Computer Data System"]
    end

    inletA --> sourceB
    inletB --> sourceB
    inletC --> sourceB
    sourceA --> sourceB
    sourceB --> sourceC
    sourceC --> accelA
    accelA --> accelB
    accelB --> sectorA
    sectorB --> sectorA
    sectorC --> sectorA
    sectorA --> detectA
    detectA --> detectB
    detectB --> detectC
    supportA -.-> sourceB
    supportA -.-> sectorA
    supportB -.-> sectorC
    supportC -.-> detectC
```

### 4.3 Detailed Functional Block Diagram — Double-Focusing Mass Spectrometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["Sample Inlet"] --> srcA["Ion Source EI or CI"]
    srcA --> accelA["Accelerator Slit S1"]
    accelA --> esaA["Electrostatic Analyzer ESA Energy Filter"]
    esaA --> magA["Magnetic Sector Momentum Filter"]
    magA --> slitA["Collector Slit S2"]
    slitA --> detA["Detector Faraday Cup or EM"]
    detA --> procA["Amplifier and ADC"]
    procA --> compA["Computer Mass Spectrum Output"]
    vacA["Vacuum 10 power -6 mbar"] -.-> srcA
    vacA -.-> esaA
    vacA -.-> magA
```

### 4.4 Schematic Ion Trajectory — Circular Path in Magnetic Sector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    entry["Ion Entry from Accelerator"] --> curvature["Curved Path of Radius r in Magnetic Field B"]
    curvature --> focus["Focused at Collector Slit S2"]
    noteText["B field perpendicular to page"] -.-> curvature
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The curved path represents the trajectory of a charged ion under the influence of a magnetic field `B` directed **into the page** (⊗ symbol). The radius `r` is determined by the ion's momentum. Heavier ions (or ions with larger m/z) travel in wider arcs; lighter ions in tighter arcs. By scanning `B` (or `V`), ions of different m/z are sequentially brought to the fixed collector slit.

### 4.5 Component Responsibilities Summary Table

| Component | Primary Function | Typical KTU-Exam Key Points |
|---|---|---|
| **Sample Inlet** | Introduce sample without breaking vacuum | Heated transfer line for GC-MS; direct probe for solids |
| **Ionization Source** | Convert neutral molecules to ions | EI: 70 eV electrons, hard ionization, fragmentation |
| **Accelerator** | Impart known kinetic energy to ions | Slit S1, potential V (1–10 kV) |
| **Magnetic Sector** | Separate ions by momentum/mass | Radius r, field B scanning |
| **Detector** | Convert ion current to electrical signal | Faraday Cup: low gain, high stability; EM: high gain |
| **Vacuum System** | Maintain mean free path > ion path length | Turbomolecular + rotary backing pump |
| **Data System** | Acquire, process, display mass spectrum | Computer with A/D converter |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:**
**Define mass spectrometry. What is meant by the term "mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)"?**

**Model Answer:**
Mass spectrometry is an analytical technique used to measure the mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) of ionized atoms or molecules, in order to determine their molecular mass, structure, and quantity. The technique involves ionization, mass separation, and detection of ions in a high-vacuum environment.

The **mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)** is defined as the mass of the ion (expressed in atomic mass units, u) divided by the number of elementary charges (z) it carries. For a singly charged ion (z = 1), m/z equals the mass number. It is the fundamental quantity measured by all mass spectrometers and forms the abscissa of the mass spectrum. **(3 marks)**

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]:**
**List any three essential components of a mass spectrometer and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Ionization Source:** Converts neutral sample molecules into ions, typically by electron impact (EI) where a hot filament emits 70 eV electrons that ionize the analyte.
2. **Mass Analyzer (e.g., Magnetic Sector):** Separates the ions based on their mass-to-charge ratio using electric or magnetic fields. In a magnetic sector, ions follow circular paths whose radii depend on m/z.
3. **Detector:** Receives the separated ions and converts the ion current into an amplified electrical signal. Examples include the Faraday cup and electron multiplier.
**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]:**

**(a)** Derive the equation $\dfrac{m}{q} = \dfrac{B^2 r^2}{2V}$ for a magnetic deflection mass spectrometer, clearly stating the underlying physical principles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A magnetic sector mass spectrometer has a sector radius of 25 cm and an accelerating voltage of 3000 V. Calculate the magnetic field required to focus the ion ⁴⁰Ar⁺. If the instrument is now used to focus ²⁰Ne⁺, calculate the new magnetic field at the same accelerating voltage. Given: mass of ⁴⁰Ar = 39.96 u, mass of ²⁰Ne = 19.99 u, e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, 1 u = 1.6605 × 10⁻²⁷ kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Step 1: A charged ion moving with velocity v perpendicular to a magnetic field B experiences a Lorentz force of magnitude F = qvB. This force acts perpendicular to the velocity at every instant, providing the centripetal force for circular motion. Therefore:

$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

Step 2: Rearranging this equation gives the radius of the circular path:

$$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$

Step 3: The ion is accelerated from rest through an electrostatic potential V, gaining kinetic energy equal to the work done by the electric field:

$$qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Therefore:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}}$$

Step 4: Substituting this velocity into the radius equation:

$$r = \frac{m}{qB}\sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}} = \frac{1}{B}\sqrt{\frac{2mV}{q}}$$

Step 5: Squaring both sides and rearranging:

$$r^2 = \frac{2mV}{qB^2}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{m}{q} = \frac{B^2 r^2}{2V}}$$

**Physical Principles Used:**
- Lorentz force on a moving charge
- Conservation of energy during acceleration
- Uniform circular motion under centripetal force

**(7 marks — 2 marks for equation setup, 3 marks for substitution and algebra, 2 marks for final equation and physical interpretation)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**For ⁴⁰Ar⁺:**

Convert mass to SI units:

$$m_{Ar} = 39.96 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 6.635 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}$$

Calculate m/q:

$$\frac{m}{q} = \frac{6.635 \times 10^{-26}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 4.142 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/C}$$

Substitute into the master equation $\dfrac{m}{q} = \dfrac{B^2 r^2}{2V}$ and solve for B:

$$B^2 = \frac{2V \cdot (m/q)}{r^2}$$

$$B^2 = \frac{2 \times 3000 \times 4.142 \times 10^{-7}}{(0.25)^2}$$

$$B^2 = \frac{2.485 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0625} = 0.03976 \text{ T}^2$$

$$B_{Ar} = \sqrt{0.03976} = 0.1994 \text{ T}$$

**B_Ar ≈ 0.199 T** **(3.5 marks)**

**For ²⁰Ne⁺:**

Convert mass to SI units:

$$m_{Ne} = 19.99 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 3.319 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}$$

$$\frac{m}{q} = \frac{3.319 \times 10^{-26}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.072 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/C}$$

$$B^2 = \frac{2 \times 3000 \times 2.072 \times 10^{-7}}{(0.25)^2}$$

$$B^2 = \frac{1.243 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0625} = 0.01989 \text{ T}^2$$

$$B_{Ne} = \sqrt{0.01989} = 0.1410 \text{ T}$$

**B_Ne ≈ 0.141 T** **(3.5 marks)**

**Verification:** The ratio of fields $\frac{B_{Ar}}{B_{Ne}} = \frac{0.1994}{0.1410} = 1.414 \approx \sqrt{\frac{39.96}{19.99}} = \sqrt{1.999} = 1.414$. This confirms the B ∝ √m relationship.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style — Internal Choice]:**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the major components of a magnetic deflection mass spectrometer. Explain the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **double focusing** in mass spectrometry. Why is it necessary, and how does the **Mattauch-Herzog geometry** achieve it? Compare its resolving power with single-focusing instruments. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram Description:**

A magnetic deflection mass spectrometer consists of the following major components:

1. **Sample Inlet System:** Introduces the sample into the ionization source. For gases, a capillary or batch inlet is used. For GC-MS coupling, a heated transfer line delivers the eluting compounds. For solids, a direct insertion probe places the sample near the ionization region.

2. **Ionization Source:** The most common is the **Electron Impact (EI) source**. A tungsten or rhenium filament is heated to ~2000°C, emitting electrons that are accelerated to 70 eV and collide with sample molecules, producing positive ions and extensive fragmentation.

3. **Accelerator and Slit S1:** The ions are extracted from the source and accelerated through a potential difference of typically 1–10 kV. Slit S1 defines a narrow ion beam with a well-defined kinetic energy qV.

4. **Magnetic Sector Analyzer:** A curved sector magnet with uniform magnetic field B over the flight path. The radius r of the circular trajectory of an ion is given by r = mv/qB. For fixed r and V, only ions of a specific m/z reach the collector slit at any given B-field setting.

5. **Collector Slit S2 and Detector:** Slit S2 is positioned at the focal point of the magnetic sector. The detector (Faraday cup for high currents, electron multiplier for low currents) converts the ion current into an amplified electrical signal.

6. **Vacuum System:** Turbomolecular pump backed by a rotary pump maintains a vacuum of 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁹ mbar throughout the ion path. This is essential to prevent ion-molecule collisions that would scatter the ions and degrade resolution.

7. **Data System:** A computer with an A/D converter records the detector signal as B is scanned, producing the mass spectrum of relative intensity vs. m/z.

**(7 marks — 1 mark per major component, with at least 2 marks for the block diagram and 1 mark for explanation of operation)**

**Part (b) — Double Focusing Concept:**

**Problem with Single Focusing:**
In a single-focusing magnetic sector, ions of the same nominal m/z but slightly different kinetic energies (due to the thermal energy spread at the source, typically 0.1–1 eV) follow paths of slightly different radii. This causes **peak broadening** and limits the resolving power to about 1000–10,000.

**Solution — Double Focusing:**
Double focusing adds an **Electrostatic Analyzer (ESA)** — a curved pair of plates held at a potential difference that selects ions based on kinetic energy rather than mass. Only ions with the correct kinetic energy (qV from the accelerator) pass through the ESA slit. These energy-purified ions then enter the magnetic sector, which separates them by momentum. The combination achieves:
- **Direction focusing** (by the magnetic sector)
- **Energy focusing** (by the ESA)

**Mattauch-Herzog Geometry:**
This is a specific arrangement where the ESA and the magnetic sector are positioned so that ions of *all* m/z values are focused simultaneously onto a **focal plane** (a flat detector plane) rather than at a single point. This allows the use of a **photographic plate** or a **multi-channel array detector**, recording the entire mass spectrum in parallel — a major advantage for high-sensitivity and rapid-scan applications.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Single Focusing | Double Focusing |
|---|---|---|
| Analyzers used | Magnetic sector only | ESA + Magnetic sector |
| Resolving power | 1,000 – 10,000 | 10,000 – 100,000+ |
| Mass accuracy | ~0.1 u | < 1 ppm |
| Detects energy spread? | No | Yes (via ESA) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Typical use | Routine GC-MS, leak detection | Exact mass, isotope ratio, structure elucidation |

**(7 marks — 2 marks for problem identification, 2 marks for ESA function, 2 marks for Mattauch-Herzog geometry, 1 mark for comparison table)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Mass Spectrometry Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert atomic mass units to kg. One mark deducted per error. KTU examiners explicitly check for `1 u = 1.6605 × 10⁻²⁷ kg`.
> 2. **Confusing m/z with m:** Always write the final answer as m/q or m/z, not just m. The charge information is essential.
> 3. **Mixing up radius r and slit width s:** The slit width in resolving power equation is `s`, not `r`. Mixing them is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Skipping the B-field direction assumption:** A magnetic sector assumes `v ⊥ B`. If the geometry is different, the equation changes. State the assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing Magnetic Sector with Quadrupole:** The equation `m/q = B²r²/2V` is **ONLY** for magnetic sector. Quadrupole MS has a completely different equation.
> 6. **No diagram = no full marks:** In Part (a) of 14-mark questions, a labeled block diagram is **mandatory**. A 2-mark deduction applies if absent.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Molecular Mass Spectrometry**

- ✅ **Mass Spectrometer ≠ Optical Spectrometer.** It measures ions, not photons. Operating principle: separate ions by m/z under high vacuum.
- ✅ **Five sequential stages:** Inlet → Ionization → Acceleration → Mass Analysis → Detection → Data.
- ✅ **Electron Impact (EI)** is the most common ionization method: 70 eV electrons, produces extensive fragmentation, library-searchable spectra.
- ✅ **Master equation:** $\dfrac{m}{q} = \dfrac{B^2 r^2}{2V}$ — derived from Lorentz force + energy conservation.
- ✅ **Magnetic field required is proportional to √m** (at fixed V, r): $B \propto \sqrt{m}$.
- ✅ **Resolving power:** $R = \dfrac{m}{\Delta m} = \dfrac{r}{s_1 + s_2}$. Larger r or smaller slits → higher R.
- ✅ **Single focusing** = magnetic sector only; **Double focusing** = ESA + magnetic sector (for high resolution and exact mass).
- ✅ **Mattauch-Herzog geometry** enables simultaneous focusing of all masses onto a focal plane (multi-channel detection).
- ✅ **Components to memorize:** Inlet, Source, Accelerator, Magnetic Sector, Detector (Faraday Cup or Electron Multiplier), Vacuum Pumps, Data System.
- ✅ **Vacuum requirement:** 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁹ mbar. Mean free path must exceed ion path length.
- ✅ **Key detectors:** Faraday Cup (low gain, ~10⁶ Ω, robust, used in IRMS) and Electron Multiplier (gain 10⁶–10⁸, fast, for trace analysis).
- ✅ **SI units are non-negotiable:** Always convert u → kg and e → C before calculation.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** GC-MS (forensics, environment), LC-MS (pharma), ICP-MS (trace metals), IRMS (isotope ratios), RGA (vacuum diagnostics).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proton Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (NMR): Nuclear Spin, Resonance Condition, Relaxation Condition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT521 - ANALYTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proton Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (NMR): Nuclear Spin, Resonance Condition, Relaxation Condition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectroscopy** is a quantum-mechanical, non-destructive analytical technique that exploits the magnetic properties of certain atomic nuclei (notably the proton, ¹H) to deduce molecular structure, chemical environment, and dynamic behaviour. The technique is founded on three pillars: **(1) Nuclear Spin**, **(2) the Resonance Condition**, and **(3) the Relaxation Condition**.

**Nuclear Spin** is an intrinsic quantum property of a nucleus, described by the **spin quantum number $I$**. It arises from the combined angular momentum of the constituent protons and neutrons. For the proton (¹H), $I = \tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Resonance Condition** is the precise condition under which an externally applied radio-frequency (RF) field can induce transitions between the nuclear spin energy levels created by a static external magnetic field $B_0$. Mathematically, it is governed by the **Larmor equation**:

$$\nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

**Relaxation Condition** describes the time-dependent recovery of the perturbed nuclear spin population back to thermal equilibrium, characterised by two first-order time constants: the **spin–lattice (longitudinal) relaxation time $T_1$** and the **spin–spin (transverse) relaxation time $T_2$**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Spinning Top in Earth's Magnetic Field**

Imagine a **spinning top** in Earth's gravitational field:
- The top is your **nucleus with spin**.
- Gravity tries to pull the axis down, but the top **precesses** (wobbles) around the vertical — this is exactly how a magnetic nucleus **precesses** around the external field $B_0$.
- If you tap the top rhythmically **at its natural precession frequency** (kick it once every precession cycle), its wobble grows dramatically — this is **resonance**.
- When you stop tapping, the top gradually returns to stable motion due to air friction and bearing friction — this is **relaxation**.

A proton in an NMR magnet behaves identically: the static $B_0$ is gravity, the RF pulse is the rhythmic tap, and the lattice provides the friction that restores equilibrium.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Property | Symbol | Value (for ¹H) | Unit |
|----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Gyromagnetic ratio | $\gamma$ | **2.675 × 10⁸** | rad T⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| Spin quantum number | $I$ | **1/2** | dimensionless |
| Planck's constant (reduced) | $\hbar$ | **1.0546 × 10⁻³⁴** | J·s |
| Nuclear magneton | $\mu_N$ | **5.051 × 10⁻²⁷** | J T⁻¹ |
| Proton magnetic moment | $\mu_p$ | **+2.7928 $\mu_N$** | J T⁻¹ |
| Field strength (60 MHz instrument) | $B_0$ | **1.41** | Tesla |
| Field strength (300 MHz instrument) | $B_0$ | **7.05** | Tesla |
| Field strength (600 MHz instrument) | $B_0$ | **14.10** | Tesla |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ¹H nucleus has $I = 1/2$ and is therefore the simplest, most studied NMR-active nucleus. Nuclei with $I = 0$ (e.g., ¹²C, ¹⁶O) are **NMR silent**.

---

## 1.4 Isotopes of Interest

| Isotope | Spin $I$ | Natural Abundance (%) | NMR Frequency at 7.05 T (MHz) |
|---------|----------|-----------------------|-------------------------------|
| ¹H | 1/2 | 99.985 | **300.13** |
| ¹³C | 1/2 | 1.108 | **75.47** |
| ¹⁵N | 1/2 | 0.37 | **30.42** |
| ¹⁹F | 1/2 | 100 | **282.40** |
| ³¹P | 1/2 | 100 | **121.49** |
| ²H (D) | 1 | 0.015 | 46.07 |
| ¹⁴N | 1 | 99.63 | 21.69 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Quadrupolar nuclei ($I \ge 1$) such as ¹⁴N and ²H produce **broad lines** because of rapid quadrupolar relaxation, while $I = 1/2$ nuclei give the **sharp, well-resolved lines** that are practically useful in structural elucidation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nuclear Spin — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Origin of Nuclear Spin

A nucleus with mass number $A$ and atomic number $Z$ possesses $Z$ protons and $(A-Z)$ neutrons. Each nucleon has an intrinsic spin of $\tfrac{1}{2}$. The **total nuclear spin angular momentum vector** $\vec{I}$ has a magnitude governed by the spin quantum number $I$:

$$\vert \vec{I} \vert = \hbar \sqrt{I(I+1)}$$

The **z-component** $I_z$ is quantised in units of $\hbar$:

$$I_z = m_I \hbar, \quad m_I = -I, -I+1, \ldots, +I-1, +I$$

giving a total of **$(2I+1)$** allowed projections.

### 2.1.2 Magnetic Moment

A spinning, charged nucleus behaves as a microscopic bar magnet. Its **magnetic dipole moment** is:

$$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \hbar \vec{I} = g_N \mu_N \vec{I}$$

where $g_N$ is the nuclear g-factor and $\mu_N$ is the nuclear magneton.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the proton have $\gamma > 0$?** The positive sign of $\gamma$ for ¹H means that its magnetic moment vector is **parallel** to its spin angular momentum vector. Many textbook derivations use this to fix the sign conventions in the Larmor equation.

### 2.1.3 Behaviour in a Static Field $B_0$

When a static magnetic field $\vec{B}_0 = B_0 \hat{z}$ is applied, the interaction energy (Zeeman interaction) is:

$$E = -\vec{\mu} \cdot \vec{B}_0 = -\gamma \hbar I_z B_0 = -\gamma \hbar m_I B_0$$

For ¹H ($I = 1/2$): the two allowed energies are

$$E_{\alpha} = -\frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2} \quad (\text{spin "up", } m_I = +1/2)$$

$$E_{\beta} = +\frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2} \quad (\text{spin "down", } m_I = -1/2)$$

The energy gap is therefore

$$\Delta E = E_{\beta} - E_{\alpha} = \gamma \hbar B_0$$

### 2.1.4 Boltzmann Population Difference

At thermal equilibrium (temperature $T$), the population ratio of the two levels follows the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$\frac{N_{\alpha}}{N_{\beta}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{k_B T}\right)$$

For a typical 300 MHz instrument ($B_0 = 7.05$ T) at 300 K:

$$\frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{k_B T} \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \implies \frac{N_{\alpha} - N_{\beta}}{N_{\alpha} + N_{\beta}} \approx 1.25 \times 10^{-5}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** The **tiny population excess** (≈ 1 in 80 000) is the fundamental reason NMR is an **inherently insensitive** technique. The net magnetisation is extremely small, and modern NMR spectrometers use signal averaging, higher fields, and cryogenic probes to overcome this.

### 2.1.5 Larmor Precession

The classical equation of motion of a magnetic moment in a field is

$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0$$

The solution is a **precession** of $\vec{\mu}$ about $\vec{B}_0$ at the **Larmor angular frequency**:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \text{(rad s}^{-1}\text{)}$$

or, in cycles per second:

$$\nu_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

---

## 2.2 Resonance Condition — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 The Selection Rule

A nucleus in the lower energy state $E_\alpha$ can absorb a photon of energy $\Delta E$ and jump to $E_\beta$ **only if** the photon frequency matches the Larmor precession frequency. The selection rule for magnetic dipole transitions is:

$$\Delta m_I = \pm 1$$

The **resonance condition** is therefore:

$$h\nu_0 = \Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0 \implies \nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

### 2.2.2 Two Practical Implementations

| Method | What is swept | What is held constant | Use case |
|--------|---------------|----------------------|----------|
| **Continuous Wave (CW)** | $B_0$ (or $\nu$) | $\nu$ (or $B_0$) | Older, slower instruments |
| **Fourier Transform (FT)** | RF pulse (broadband excitation) | $B_0$ | Modern, dominant technique |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In an FT-NMR spectrometer, a short (~5–10 µs) RF pulse at $\nu_0$ is applied. Because the pulse is broadband, **all** protons in the sample resonate simultaneously, producing a **free induction decay (FID)** in the time domain, which is Fourier transformed to give the familiar frequency-domain spectrum.

### 2.2.3 Chemical Shift — Why NMR is Useful

The effective field at a proton is not exactly $B_0$ but is **shielded** by the local electronic environment:

$$B_{\text{eff}} = B_0 (1 - \sigma)$$

where $\sigma$ is the **shielding constant**. The resonance condition becomes:

$$\nu = \frac{\gamma B_0 (1 - \sigma)}{2\pi}$$

The **chemical shift** $\delta$, expressed in **ppm**, is field-independent:

$$\delta = \frac{\nu - \nu_{\text{ref}}}{\nu_{\text{spectrometer}}} \times 10^6 \text{ ppm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Because $\delta$ is in ppm, a peak at, say, 7.26 ppm appears at exactly 7.26 ppm on a 60 MHz or a 600 MHz instrument, although the absolute frequency separation differs. This universality makes NMR data portable across laboratories.

---

## 2.3 Relaxation Condition — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.3.1 Why Relaxation is Needed

After an RF pulse, the net magnetisation vector $\vec{M}$ is tipped away from the $z$-axis. **Two coupled processes** bring it back to equilibrium:

1. **Longitudinal (spin–lattice) relaxation** — $T_1$: Recovery of $M_z$ toward $M_0$ by transferring energy to the surrounding molecular lattice (thermal reservoir).
2. **Transverse (spin–spin) relaxation** — $T_2$: Decay of $M_x$ and $M_y$ due to dephasing of individual spins in the $xy$-plane.

### 2.3.2 Bloch Equations

The phenomenological equations governing the time evolution of the magnetisation vector $\vec{M} = (M_x, M_y, M_z)$ in a rotating frame at the Larmor frequency are the **Bloch equations**:

$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = -\frac{M_x}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = -\frac{M_y}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \frac{M_0 - M_z}{T_1}$$

### 2.3.3 Solutions (with $M_z(0) = 0$, $M_{x,y}(0) = M_0$ for a 90° pulse)

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/T_1} \right)$$

$$M_x(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \cos(\Omega t)$$

$$M_y(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \sin(\Omega t)$$

where $\Omega$ is the offset frequency from the rotating frame.

### 2.3.4 Relation between Linewidth and $T_2$

The FID decays as $e^{-t/T_2}$, so the Fourier-transformed line has a **Lorentzian shape** with full width at half maximum:

$$\Delta\nu_{1/2} = \frac{1}{\pi T_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Short $T_2$ ⟹ broad peaks; long $T_2$ ⟹ sharp peaks. Small, rapidly tumbling molecules (e.g., in low-viscosity solvents) have long $T_2$ and give sharp lines. Large, slowly tumbling molecules (e.g., proteins, polymers) have short $T_2$ and give broad lines — this is the principle behind **MRI contrast** and **NOE experiments**.

### 2.3.5 Typical $T_1$ and $T_2$ Values

| Sample | $T_1$ (s) | $T_2$ (s) |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Water (deoxygenated) | 3–4 | 3–4 |
| Water (oxygenated) | 0.4–0.6 | 0.4–0.6 |
| Liquid chloroform-d | 20–30 | 20–30 |
| Solid polymers | 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵ | 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁶ |
| In vivo tissue (fat) | 0.2 | 0.07 |
| In vivo tissue (muscle) | 0.7 | 0.04 |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise these core equations — they account for ~80% of KTU exam marks on this topic.**

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|----------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\vert \vec{I} \vert = \hbar \sqrt{I(I+1)}$ | Nuclear spin angular momentum magnitude | J s |
| 2 | $I_z = m_I \hbar$ | z-component quantisation | J s |
| 3 | $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \hbar \vec{I}$ | Magnetic moment relation | J T⁻¹ |
| 4 | $E = -\gamma \hbar m_I B_0$ | Zeeman energy | J |
| 5 | $\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0$ | Energy gap for ¹H | J |
| 6 | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Larmor angular frequency | rad s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ | **Larmor frequency (resonance condition)** | Hz |
| 8 | $N_\alpha / N_\beta = \exp(\gamma \hbar B_0 / k_B T)$ | Boltzmann populations | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\delta = (\nu - \nu_{\text{ref}})/\nu_{\text{spec}} \times 10^6$ | Chemical shift | ppm |

| 12 | $\Delta \nu_{1/2} = 1/(\pi T_2)$ | Linewidth from $T_2$ | Hz |

**Useful numerical identities for KTU problems:**

- $\gamma_{^1H} = 2.675 \times 10^8$ rad T⁻¹ s⁻¹
- $\gamma_{^1H} / 2\pi = 42.577$ MHz T⁻¹
- ¹H at 1.41 T ⟹ $\nu_0 = 60$ MHz
- ¹H at 7.05 T ⟹ $\nu_0 = 300$ MHz
- ¹H at 14.10 T ⟹ $\nu_0 = 600$ MHz

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Splitting in a Magnetic Field

**Objective:** Starting from the magnetic moment definition, derive the energy difference between the two spin states of ¹H.

**Step 1.** Define the magnetic moment in terms of the spin angular momentum:

$$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L} = \gamma \hbar \vec{I}$$

**Step 2.** Write the interaction (Zeeman) energy of this moment with the external field $\vec{B}_0 = B_0 \hat{z}$:

$$E = -\vec{\mu} \cdot \vec{B}_0 = -\mu_z B_0 = -\gamma \hbar I_z B_0$$

**Step 3.** Substitute the quantised form $I_z = m_I$:

$$E_{m_I} = -\gamma \hbar m_I B_0$$

**Step 4.** For ¹H with $I = 1/2$, the allowed values of $m_I$ are $+\tfrac{1}{2}$ and $-\tfrac{1}{2}$. Hence:

$$E_{m_I = +1/2} = -\gamma \hbar \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) B_0 = -\frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2}$$

$$E_{m_I = -1/2} = -\gamma \hbar \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) B_0 = +\frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2}$$

**Step 5.** The energy gap is the difference:

$$\Delta E = E_{-1/2} - E_{+1/2} = \frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2} - \left(-\frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2}\right) = \gamma \hbar B_0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0$ — this is the **fundamental energy quantum** that drives all NMR phenomena. The factor of 2 that appears in the "splitting" intuition is now explicit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Larmor Equation (Resonance Condition)

**Objective:** Derive the resonance frequency from the classical equation of motion of the magnetic moment.

**Step 1.** A magnetic dipole in a magnetic field experiences a torque $\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0$.

**Step 2.** By Newton's second law for angular momentum $\vec{L}$:

$$\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0$$

**Step 3.** Since $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$:

$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \left(\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0\right)$$

**Step 4.** With $\vec{B}_0 = B_0 \hat{z}$, expand the cross product in Cartesian components:

$$\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0 = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{x} & \hat{y} & \hat{z} \\ \mu_x & \mu_y & \mu_z \\ 0 & 0 & B_0 \end{vmatrix} = (\mu_y B_0)\hat{x} - (\mu_x B_0)\hat{y} + 0\hat{z}$$

**Step 5.** Therefore the three scalar equations are:

$$\frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma B_0 \mu_y$$

$$\frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma B_0 \mu_x$$

$$\frac{d\mu_z}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 6.** Differentiate the first equation with respect to time and substitute the second:

$$\frac{d^2 \mu_x}{dt^2} = \gamma B_0 \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = \gamma B_0 (-\gamma B_0 \mu_x) = -(\gamma B_0)^2 \mu_x$$

**Step 7.** This is the **simple harmonic oscillator equation**:

$$\frac{d^2 \mu_x}{dt^2} + \omega_0^2 \mu_x = 0, \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

**Step 8.** The general solution is sinusoidal precession:

$$\mu_x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t), \quad \mu_y(t) = -A \sin(\omega_0 t)$$

**Step 9.** Convert angular frequency to linear frequency (cycles per second) using $\omega_0 = 2\pi \nu_0$:

$$\nu_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ — the **Larmor equation**. The precession is a classical phenomenon; the quantum interpretation is that the photon energy $h\nu_0$ exactly matches $\Delta E$ from Section 3.1.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boltzmann Population Difference

**Objective:** Compute the population difference ratio as a function of field strength and temperature.

**Step 1.** From the Boltzmann distribution, the probability of finding a spin in level $E_i$ is:

$$P_i = \frac{e^{-E_i / k_B T}}{Z}, \quad Z = \sum_j e^{-E_j / k_B T}$$

**Step 2.** For the two-level proton system, the partition function is:

$$Z = e^{-E_\alpha / k_B T} + e^{-E_\beta / k_B T} = e^{+\gamma \hbar B_0 / 2k_B T} + e^{-\gamma \hbar B_0 / 2k_B T} = 2 \cosh\!\left(\frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 3.** The population difference is:

$$\Delta N = N_\alpha - N_\beta = N_{\text{total}} \cdot \frac{e^{+\gamma \hbar B_0/2k_B T} - e^{-\gamma \hbar B_0/2k_B T}}{e^{+\gamma \hbar B_0/2k_B T} + e^{-\gamma \hbar B_0/2k_B T}}$$

**Step 4.** Recognise the hyperbolic-tangent identity:

$$\Delta N = N_{\text{total}} \tanh\!\left(\frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 5.** For $\gamma \hbar B_0 \ll 2 k_B T$ (always true for NMR), use the small-argument expansion $\tanh(x) \approx x$:

$$\Delta N \approx N_{\text{total}} \cdot \frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2 k_B T}$$

**Step 6.** Evaluate numerically for ¹H at 7.05 T and 300 K:

$$\frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2 k_B T} = \frac{(2.675 \times 10^8)(1.0546 \times 10^{-34})(7.05)}{2 (1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)} \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\Delta N \approx 0.000025 \times N_{\text{total}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Even on a 600 MHz instrument, the excess is only about 1 in 16 000. This is why **hyperpolarisation techniques** (DNP, optical pumping) are an active research frontier — they can boost $\Delta N$ by factors of 1000×, opening up new applications in metabolic imaging and low-γ nuclei.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Solution of the Bloch Equations (Relaxation)

**Objective:** Derive $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ from the Bloch equation for longitudinal relaxation.

**Step 1.** Start with the longitudinal Bloch equation:

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \frac{M_0 - M_z}{T_1}$$

**Step 2.** Rearrange:

$$\frac{dM_z}{M_0 - M_z} = \frac{dt}{T_1}$$

**Step 3.** Integrate both sides from $t = 0$ (where $M_z = M_z(0)$) to $t = t$:

$$\int_{M_z(0)}^{M_z(t)} \frac{dM_z'}{M_0 - M_z'} = \frac{1}{T_1} \int_0^t dt'$$

**Step 4.** The integral on the left is $-\ln(M_0 - M_z) \,\Big|_{M_z(0)}^{M_z(t)}$:

$$-\ln\!\left(\frac{M_0 - M_z(t)}{M_0 - M_z(0)}\right) = \frac{t}{T_1}$$

**Step 5.** Exponentiate:

$$\frac{M_0 - M_z(t)}{M_0 - M_z(0)} = e^{-t/T_1}$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $M_z(t)$:

$$M_z(t) = M_0 - (M_0 - M_z(0)) e^{-t/T_1}$$

**Step 7.** For a 90° pulse that tips the magnetisation entirely into the $xy$-plane, $M_z(0) = 0$, and:

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/T_1} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** At $t = 0$, $M_z = 0$ (no longitudinal magnetisation). As $t \to \infty$, $M_z \to M_0$ (full recovery). At $t = T_1$, $M_z = M_0 (1 - 1/e) \approx 0.632 M_0$. Therefore, **$T_1$ is the time required for the magnetisation to recover to 63.2% of its equilibrium value**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Linewidth from $T_2$

**Objective:** Show that a decaying sinusoidal FID produces a Lorentzian line of width $\Delta\nu_{1/2} = 1/(\pi T_2)$.

**Step 1.** The time-domain FID signal from a single resonance at offset $\Omega$ from the rotating-frame Larmor frequency is:

$$S(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \left[ \cos(\Omega t) + i \sin(\Omega t) \right] = M_0 \, e^{i\Omega t} e^{-t/T_2}$$

**Step 2.** Take the complex Fourier transform to obtain the frequency-domain spectrum $F(\omega)$:

$$F(\omega) = \int_0^{\infty} M_0 \, e^{i\Omega t} e^{-t/T_2} \, e^{-i\omega t} \, dt = M_0 \int_0^{\infty} e^{-(1/T_2 + i(\omega - \Omega)) t} \, dt$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate the integral (it is a standard exponential integral):

$$F(\omega) = \frac{M_0}{\dfrac{1}{T_2} + i(\omega - \Omega)}$$

**Step 4.** The absorption-mode spectrum is the real part of $F$ (after suitable phase correction):

$$\text{Re}[F(\omega)] = \frac{M_0 / T_2}{(1/T_2)^2 + (\omega - \Omega)^2}$$

**Step 5.** This is a Lorentzian function. Its full width at half maximum (FWHM) is found by setting the denominator equal to twice its minimum value. The minimum occurs at $\omega = \Omega$, with value $(1/T_2)^2$. Half-maximum requires:

$$(1/T_2)^2 + (\Delta\omega_{1/2})^2 = 2 (1/T_2)^2$$

$$\implies (\Delta\omega_{1/2})^2 = (1/T_2)^2 \implies \Delta\omega_{1/2} = 1/T_2$$

**Step 6.** Convert to Hz using $\omega = 2\pi\nu$:

$$\Delta\nu_{1/2} = \frac{1}{2\pi T_2} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{\pi T_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** The **narrower** the line, the **longer** $T_2$. This is why shimming (homogenising $B_0$) is critical — any residual field inhomogeneity shortens the *observed* $T_2^*$ and broadens lines. Spectrometers report $T_2^*$ (with inhomogeneity) and $T_2$ (intrinsic, from spin-echo sequences).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Master Equation | Physical Meaning | Engineering Handle |
|---------|-----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Spin | $I$ determines $2I+1$ levels | Quantum number of nucleus | Choose isotope |
| Energy splitting | $\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0$ | Zeeman gap | Increase $B_0$ |
| Resonance | $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ | Photon frequency | Tune RF to match |
| Population | $\tanh(\gamma \hbar B_0 / 2k_B T)$ | Net magnetisation | Increase $B_0$ or lower $T$ |
| $T_1$ | $M_z = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ | Lattice coupling | Viscosity, temperature |
| $T_2$ | $M_{x,y} = M_0 e^{-t/T_2}$ | Spin dephasing | Molecular tumbling, $B_0$ homogeneity |
| Linewidth | $\Delta\nu_{1/2} = 1/\pi T_2$ | Resolution limit | Shimming, smaller $\Delta B_0$ |
