# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define nuclear spin. Why is ¹H NMR-active while ¹²C is NMR-silent?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Nuclear spin** is an intrinsic quantum-mechanical property of a nucleus arising from the combined spins of its protons and neutrons. It is described by the spin quantum number $I$, which can take values $0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots$
- The number of allowed spin projections is $2I+1$, and only nuclei with $I > 0$ possess a magnetic moment and are **NMR-active**.
- For ¹H: number of protons = 1, number of neutrons = 0, both have $I = 1/2$ and combine to give $I = 1/2$. Therefore $I > 0$ and ¹H is **NMR-active**.
- For ¹²C: number of protons = 6, number of neutrons = 6, spins pair up to give $I = 0$. Therefore ¹²C is **NMR-silent**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the resonance condition for a proton in a magnetic field.**

**Model Answer:**

- The resonance condition states that an external radio-frequency (RF) field of frequency $\nu_0$ can induce transitions between nuclear Zeeman levels only when its photon energy matches the energy gap:

$$h\nu_0 = \Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0$$

- Simplifying yields the **Larmor equation**:

$$\nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

- For ¹H with $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8$ rad T⁻¹ s⁻¹, in a 7.05 T field, $\nu_0 \approx 300$ MHz.
- When this condition is satisfied, the protons absorb RF energy and a signal is detected — this is the **NMR signal**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has internal choice — students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Larmor equation for proton NMR starting from the torque on a magnetic moment in a static field.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the Larmor frequency of ¹H and ¹³C nuclei in a 11.74 T superconducting magnet. Given $\gamma_{^1H} = 2.675 \times 10^8$ rad T⁻¹ s⁻¹ and $\gamma_{^13C} = 6.728 \times 10^7$ rad T⁻¹ s⁻¹.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of Larmor Equation [7 Marks]**

**(Step 1, 2 marks):** A magnetic dipole moment $\vec{\mu}$ in a field $\vec{B}_0$ experiences a torque:

$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0$$

Newton's second law for angular momentum gives $\dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{\tau}$, and since $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$:

$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \left( \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0 \right)$$

**(Step 2, 2 marks):** With $\vec{B}_0 = B_0 \hat{z}$, expand the cross product:

$$\frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma B_0 \mu_y, \quad \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma B_0 \mu_x, \quad \frac{d\mu_z}{dt} = 0$$

**(Step 3, 2 marks):** Differentiating the first equation and substituting the second:

$$\frac{d^2 \mu_x}{dt^2} = -(\gamma B_0)^2 \mu_x$$

This is a SHM equation with angular frequency $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$. The solution is precession at the **Larmor angular frequency**.

**(Step 4, 1 mark):** Converting to linear frequency:

$$\nu_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]**

**For ¹H:**

$$\nu_{^1H} = \frac{\gamma_{^1H} B_0}{2\pi} = \frac{(2.675 \times 10^8)(11.74)}{2\pi}$$

$$\nu_{^1H} = \frac{3.140 \times 10^9}{6.2832} = 4.998 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} \approx 500 \text{ MHz}$$

**For ¹³C:**

$$\nu_{^13C} = \frac{\gamma_{^13C} B_0}{2\pi} = \frac{(6.728 \times 10^7)(11.74)}{2\pi}$$

$$\nu_{^13C} = \frac{7.898 \times 10^8}{6.2832} = 1.257 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} \approx 125.7 \text{ MHz}$$

**Conclusion:** This magnet is a **500 MHz ¹H instrument** and gives a ¹³C frequency of ~125.7 MHz. The ratio $\gamma_{^1H}/\gamma_{^13C} \approx 3.976$ matches the textbook value exactly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concepts of **longitudinal ($T_1$) and transverse ($T_2$) relaxation** in NMR with the help of the Bloch equations. State the physical mechanisms responsible for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a sample, $T_1 = 1.5$ s and $T_2 = 0.2$ s. Calculate (i) the expected linewidth (in Hz) and (ii) the time taken for the longitudinal magnetisation to recover to 90% of its equilibrium value.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Explanation of $T_1$ and $T_2$ [7 Marks]**

(2 marks) After an RF pulse perturbs the magnetisation, two distinct processes restore equilibrium:
- **$T_1$ — Spin–Lattice (Longitudinal) Relaxation:** Recovery of $M_z$ toward $M_0$ by transfer of energy from the excited nuclei to the surrounding molecular lattice (the thermal reservoir). The mechanism requires fluctuations at the Larmor frequency, which occur via **molecular tumbling, dipole–dipole interactions, and chemical shift anisotropy**. Liquids with small, rapidly tumbling molecules typically have long $T_1$ (seconds), while large biomolecules have short $T_1$ (milliseconds).

- **$T_2$ — Spin–Spin (Transverse) Relaxation:** Decay of $M_x$ and $M_y$ due to dephasing of precessing spins caused by **local magnetic field fluctuations from neighbouring spins**. The mechanism does not involve net energy transfer; it is a purely **entropic** process. $T_2 \le T_1$ always.

(2 marks) The Bloch equations in the rotating frame are:

$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = -\frac{M_x}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = -\frac{M_y}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \frac{M_0 - M_z}{T_1}$$

(1 mark) After a 90° pulse, the solutions are:

$$M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1}), \quad M_{x,y}(t) = M_0 e^{-t/T_2}$$

(1 mark) The FID is the observable $M_{x,y}(t)$, which decays with $T_2$. In the frequency domain, the linewidth is $\Delta\nu_{1/2} = 1/(\pi T_2)$.

(1 mark) Real-world note: $T_1$ governs the **repetition delay** between successive scans (you must wait ~$5T_1$ for full recovery). $T_2$ governs the **acquisition time** and the achievable **spectral resolution**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations [7 Marks]**

**(i) Linewidth [3 marks]:**

$$\Delta\nu_{1/2} = \frac{1}{\pi T_2} = \frac{1}{\pi \times 0.2} = \frac{1}{0.6283}$$

$$\Delta\nu_{1/2} = 1.59 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Time to 90% recovery [4 marks]:**

The condition is $M_z(t)/M_0 = 0.90$:

$$\frac{M_z(t)}{M_0} = 1 - e^{-t/T_1} = 0.90$$

$$e^{-t/T_1} = 0.10$$

$$-\frac{t}{T_1} = \ln(0.10) = -2.3026$$

$$t = 2.3026 \times T_1 = 2.3026 \times 1.5 \text{ s}$$

$$t = 3.45 \text{ s}$$

**Conclusion:** The expected linewidth is approximately 1.59 Hz, and the longitudinal magnetisation recovers to 90% of its equilibrium value in about 3.45 s. For full recovery (> 99%), one must wait at least $5T_1 = 7.5$ s — this is the minimum recommended repetition delay in a standard 1D ¹H experiment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Sign error in the Larmor equation:** Many students write $\omega_0 = 2\pi\gamma B_0$ instead of $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$. Marks are deducted immediately.
> 2. **Confusing Hz and rad/s:** $\omega_0$ is in rad s⁻¹, $\nu_0$ is in Hz. Always divide $\omega_0$ by $2\pi$ to get Hz.
> 3. **Forgetting units in Boltzmann ratio:** The exponent in $\exp(\gamma \hbar B_0 / k_B T)$ is dimensionless; the units must be J/J. KTU students often forget to convert eV to J, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing $T_1$ and $T_2$:** $T_1 \ge T_2$ always. If a calculation gives $T_1 < T_2$, the answer is wrong by physics.
> 5. **Chemical shift vs. resonance frequency:** Resonance frequency is in Hz (depends on $B_0$); chemical shift is in ppm (field-independent). Don't confuse them.
> 6. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in energy splitting:** The two levels are at $\pm \gamma \hbar B_0/2$, so the splitting is $\gamma \hbar B_0$ — students often write $\gamma \hbar B_0/2$.
> 7. **Sign of gyromagnetic ratio:** For ¹H, $\gamma > 0$ (precession is counter-clockwise viewed from +z). For ¹⁵N, $\gamma < 0$ (clockwise). KTU may test this in a 3-mark question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

### A. Nuclear Spin

- ¹H has $I = 1/2$, giving **two** Zeeman levels ($m_I = +1/2$ and $-1/2$).
- Nuclei with $I = 0$ are NMR silent (¹²C, ¹⁶O, ²⁸Si).
- Quadrupolar nuclei ($I \ge 1$, e.g., ¹⁴N, ²H) give **broad** lines.
- The magnetic moment: $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \hbar \vec{I}$ (units: J T⁻¹).
- The gyromagnetic ratio is the fingerprint of each nucleus.

### B. Resonance Condition

- **Master equation:** $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ — the Larmor equation.
- For ¹H, $\gamma/2\pi = 42.577$ MHz T⁻¹.
- Resonant absorption requires $h\nu_{\text{RF}} = \gamma \hbar B_0$.
- CW method: sweep $B_0$ at fixed $\nu_{\text{RF}}$.
- FT method: short broadband RF pulse excites all frequencies simultaneously; FID is Fourier transformed.
- The chemical shift $\delta$ in ppm is field-independent: $\delta = (\nu - \nu_{\text{ref}}) / \nu_0 \times 10^6$.

### C. Relaxation Condition

- Two coupled processes: $T_1$ (longitudinal, energy loss) and $T_2$ (transverse, dephasing).
- **Bloch equations** govern time evolution in the rotating frame.
- After a 90° pulse: $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$; $M_{x,y}(t) = M_0 e^{-t/T_2}$.
- **Always:** $T_1 \ge T_2$.
- Linewidth–$T_2$ relation: $\Delta\nu_{1/2} = 1/(\pi T_2)$.
- $T_1$ sets the **repetition delay** in pulsed experiments ($5T_1$ rule).
- $T_2$ sets the **spectral resolution**.
- Saturation occurs if repetition delay is too short.

### D. Useful Numerical Conversions (Memorise)

- ¹H at 1.41 T ⟹ 60 MHz
- ¹H at 4.70 T ⟹ 200 MHz
- ¹H at 7.05 T ⟹ 300 MHz
- ¹H at 11.74 T ⟹ 500 MHz
- ¹H at 14.10 T ⟹ 600 MHz
- ¹H at 21.14 T ⟹ 900 MHz

### E. Boltzmann Population Facts

- Excess population $\propto B_0 / T$.
- At 300 K, 7.05 T: ~25 ppm excess in ¹H levels.
- Higher field = more signal, better resolution, better sensitivity.
- Hyperpolarisation techniques (DNP, PHIP) can boost this by 10³–10⁵×.

### F. KTU Favourite Question Patterns

- "Derive the Larmor equation from first principles." (7–10 marks)
- "Calculate the Larmor frequency for nucleus X at field Y." (3–7 marks)
- "Explain the difference between $T_1$ and $T_2$ with the Bloch equations." (7 marks)
- "A sample has $T_1$ = X, $T_2$ = Y. Find the linewidth and recovery time." (7 marks)
- "Why is ¹³C NMR less sensitive than ¹H NMR?" (3 marks) — Answer: lower $\gamma$ and 1.1% natural abundance.
- "What is the chemical shift and why is it expressed in ppm?" (3–7 marks)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Gas Analysers, pH Meters, Conductivity Meters, Dissolved Oxygen Meters, Sodium Analyser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT521 - ANALYTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Industrial Gas Analysers, pH Meters, Conductivity Meters, Dissolved Oxygen Meters, Sodium Analyser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Gas Analysers

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Industrial gas analysers are on-line, continuous-monitoring instruments used to determine the quantitative composition of gaseous mixtures in process industries (power plants, chemical refineries, cement plants, steel plants, and environmental emission stacks). They operate on physical principles such as **thermal conductivity**, **paramagnetism**, **infrared absorption**, and **electrochemical (zirconia-cell) reaction** to deliver real-time concentration readouts of critical species like $O_2$, $CO_2$, $CO$, $SO_2$, $NO_x$, and $H_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** The four most frequently examined gas analysers are: (i) Thermal Conductivity Analyser, (ii) Paramagnetic Oxygen Analyser, (iii) Infrared Gas Analyser, and (iv) Zirconia Oxygen Analyser.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine your nose trying to "smell" what is in the air. It cannot give you a number, but a gas analyser can. Think of an industrial gas analyser as an **"electronic nose"** — but instead of chemical receptors, it uses physical properties. For example, oxygen is *magnetic* (most gases are not), so a magnet can "pull" oxygen molecules, and the analyser measures how strongly it gets pulled — the more oxygen, the stronger the pull, and that strength becomes a number on the screen. It is like weighing sugar with a magnet: the heavier the bag, the more sugar inside.

---

## 1.2 pH Meters

**Definition:**
A pH meter is a high-impedance electronic voltmeter that measures the **electromotive force (EMF)** developed across a glass membrane electrode immersed in a solution, and converts this potential into the negative logarithm of the hydrogen-ion activity, defined as $pH = -\log_{10}(a_{H^+})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Standard Metric:** The standard reference temperature for pH measurement is **25 °C (298.15 K)**, and the Nernst slope is **0.05916 V per pH unit** at this temperature.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of pH as a *thermometer for acidity*. Just as a thermometer measures how hot or cold water is, a pH meter measures how acidic or basic a solution is. The "temperature" of acidity is measured using two electrodes (one is a *reference* like a steady anchor, the other a *sensor* like a thermometer probe). The voltage difference between them is translated into a pH number. **pH 7 = neutral** (pure water), **pH < 7 = acidic** (lemon juice ≈ 2), **pH > 7 = basic** (bleach ≈ 13).

---

## 1.3 Conductivity Meters

**Definition:**
A conductivity meter measures the **ability of an aqueous solution to carry an electric current**, quantified as electrical conductance $G$ (in Siemens) or its normalized form, electrical conductivity $\kappa$ (in S/cm or µS/cm), using an AC-excited electrode cell (conductivity cell) immersed in the solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** Conductivity is the reciprocal of resistivity, $\kappa = 1/\rho$, and for a cell of constant $K_{cell} = l/A$, we obtain $\kappa = K_{cell} \cdot G$, where $K_{cell}$ is the **cell constant** in cm⁻¹.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a solution as a *highway for ions*. If you dissolve salt in water, you create tiny charged vehicles (ions) that can drive through the liquid and complete a circuit. The more salt you dissolve, the more "vehicles" on the road, and the easier electricity flows. A conductivity meter is essentially a *traffic counter* for these charged particles. Pure water = empty road (low conductivity ≈ 0.055 µS/cm), seawater = rush hour (≈ 50,000 µS/cm).

---

## 1.4 Dissolved Oxygen (DO) Meters

**Definition:**
A dissolved oxygen meter is an electrochemical instrument that determines the concentration of molecular oxygen ($O_2$) dissolved in a liquid (typically water in boilers, rivers, fermentation broths, or aquaculture tanks) by measuring the diffusion-limited reduction current at a noble-metal cathode separated from the sample by an **oxygen-permeable membrane** (Clark-type design).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Standard:** DO concentration is reported in **mg/L (ppm)**, with the temperature-corrected saturation value at 25 °C in air-equilibrated water equal to **8.26 mg/L** at 1 atm.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a fish in a fish tank — it breathes oxygen dissolved in water, not oxygen bubbles. A DO meter is like a *tiny artificial gill* that pulls $O_2$ through a thin plastic skin and "eats" it electrochemically. The more oxygen in the water, the faster the meter "eats," and the larger the electrical current — which is displayed as a number. **High DO = healthy water** for fish; **low DO = dead zone**.

---

## 1.5 Sodium Analyser

**Definition:**
A sodium analyser is a specialized ion-selective potentiometric instrument used to measure trace-level sodium-ion concentration ($Na^+$) in high-purity water (boiler feedwater, steam condensate, semiconductor rinse water) using a **sodium-responsive glass membrane electrode** paired with a reference electrode, with the EMF obeying a modified Nernst equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Sodium analysers typically operate in the range **0.01 ppb to 200 ppm** with a Nernstian slope of approximately **0.059 V per decade** at 25 °C when properly calibrated with ionic-strength-adjustment buffers.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of sodium ions as **silent troublemakers** in a steam power plant. Even 1 part per billion of sodium in boiler feedwater can cause turbine blade corrosion costing millions. A sodium analyser is a *highly sensitive sodium-sniffing electrode* — so sensitive it can detect sodium at concentrations lower than a pinch of salt dissolved in an Olympic swimming pool. It is the equivalent of a *metal detector at the beach* that can find a single coin buried deep in the sand.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Industrial Gas Analysers — Four Major Variants

### 2.1.1 Thermal Conductivity Analyser (TCA)

- **Principle:** Different gases conduct heat at different rates. The sample gas flows over a heated filament (typically platinum or tungsten); the rate of heat loss (and hence filament resistance $R$) depends on the gas's thermal conductivity $\lambda$.
- **Construction:** Four filaments form a **Wheatstone bridge**; two are exposed to the sample, two to a reference gas (usually sealed nitrogen or air).
- **Output:** The bridge unbalance voltage $\Delta V$ is proportional to the difference in thermal conductivities, which correlates with concentration.
- **Application:** Binary gas mixtures such as $H_2$ in $N_2$, $CO_2$ in $N_2$, $SO_2$ in air, and $CH_4$ in natural gas.

### 2.1.2 Paramagnetic Oxygen Analyser

- **Principle:** Oxygen is **strongly paramagnetic** ($\chi_{O_2} = +3449 \times 10^{-6}$ cgs), while most common gases (N₂, CO₂, H₂) are **weakly diamagnetic**. The magnetic susceptibility difference is exploited.
- **Construction:** A dumbbell-shaped test body (a small glass sphere filled with $N_2$) is suspended in a non-uniform magnetic field; oxygen molecules are drawn into the field, displacing the test body. The displacement is sensed optically or by a feedback coil.
- **Output:** Deflection angle or restoring current ∝ $p_{O_2}$ (partial pressure of oxygen).
- **Application:** Continuous $O_2$ measurement in flue gas, combustion control, and medical gas blending.

### 2.1.3 Infrared (IR) Gas Analyser

- **Principle:** Polyatomic gases ($CO$, $CO_2$, $CH_4$, $SO_2$, $NO$, $N_2O$) absorb specific IR wavelengths due to **molecular vibrational-rotational transitions**. Absorption follows the **Beer–Lambert law**.
- **Construction:** IR source → sample cell → narrow-band optical filter → IR detector (thermopile, pyroelectric, or photoacoustic). A **chopper wheel** modulates the beam at a fixed frequency (e.g., 5–25 Hz) for AC amplification.
- **Application:** Stack emission monitoring, automotive exhaust analysis, greenhouse-gas monitoring, breath analysers.

### 2.1.4 Zirconia Oxygen Analyser

- **Principle:** A **calcia-stabilized zirconia (CSZ) ceramic** becomes an oxygen-ion conductor above ~600 °C. The EMF generated between two sides exposed to different $p_{O_2}$ values follows the **Nernst equation for oxygen**.
- **Application:** Combustion control in boilers, furnaces, and car engines (lambda sensor); measurement range from 1 ppm to 100 % O₂.

### 2.1.5 Chemiluminescence Analyser (NOₓ family)

- **Principle:** The reaction $NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2^* + O_2$, followed by $NO_2^* \rightarrow NO_2 + h\nu$ (light at 600–3000 nm), produces luminescence intensity proportional to NO concentration. $NO_2$ is first converted to NO by a thermal converter for total $NO_x$ measurement.
- **Application:** Continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS) for $NO_x$ in stacks.

---

## 2.2 pH Meters — Electrochemical Foundations

- **Combination Electrode:** A single probe houses both the **glass (indicator) electrode** and the **reference electrode** (typically Ag/AgCl with saturated KCl filling solution) connected via a porous **salt bridge / liquid junction**.
- **Nernst Equation for pH Electrode:**

$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \cdot pH = E^0_{cell} - 0.05916 \cdot pH \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

- **Calibration:** Two-point calibration using buffer solutions of known pH (commonly pH 4.00, 7.00, and 10.00 NIST-traceable buffers) is mandatory to determine the slope efficiency (ideally 95–102 %) and the offset $E^0_{cell}$.
- **Temperature Compensation:** The electrode slope is temperature-dependent; a built-in or external Pt100/Pt1000 RTD provides automatic compensation. Modern "smart" pH meters use the **Debye–Hückel** algorithm to correct buffer pH for temperature.

---

## 2.3 Conductivity Meters — Measurement Cells

- **Conductivity Cell Types:** Two-electrode (DIP-style), four-electrode (for high conductivity and polarization-free measurement), and toroidal (inductive, for harsh/soiling liquids).
- **Cell Constant:** Defined as $K_{cell} = l/A$ where $l$ = distance between electrodes, $A$ = electrode area, with units of **cm⁻¹**. Standard cells: $K = 0.1$ (pure water), $1.0$ (general), $10$ (concentrated solutions).
- **Polarization Effect:** At low conductivities, DC excitation causes ion depletion at electrodes → use **AC excitation** at 50 Hz to 10 kHz.
- **Temperature Compensation:** Conductivity has a strong temperature coefficient, typically **+2 %/°C** (linear) or following the NaCl ISO 7888 standard curve.

---

## 2.4 Dissolved Oxygen Meters — Clark Electrode

- **Construction:** A Pt (or Au) cathode and Ag/AgCl anode are separated from the sample by an **O₂-permeable Teflon (FEP) membrane** held by an O-ring. A thin electrolyte film (KCl solution) fills the gap.
- **Polarographic (Clark) Mode:** A fixed polarizing voltage of **~0.6 to 0.8 V** is applied between the cathode and anode. $O_2$ diffuses through the membrane, is reduced at the cathode, generating a current proportional to $O_2$ flux:

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$

- **Galvanic Mode:** No external voltage is applied; the cell uses dissimilar metals (Pb anode, Ag cathode) and is self-polarizing.
- **Calibration:** Saturated air (100 % saturation) and zero-O₂ solution (5 % Na₂SO₃ + trace CoCl₂ catalyst) for two-point calibration.

---

## 2.5 Sodium Analyser — Ion-Selective Electrode

- **Glass Membrane Composition:** A specialized lithia-alumina-silica glass (e.g., $11\%\,Na_2O$–$18\%\,Al_2O_3$–$71\%\,SiO_2$) provides high selectivity for $Na^+$ over $H^+$ and $K^+$.
- **Reference Electrode:** Typically a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) or double-junction Ag/AgCl with alkaline filling solution to prevent $Na^+$ contamination.
- **ISA Buffer (Ionic Strength Adjuster):** A basic amine buffer (e.g., diisopropylamine + HCHO) raises pH to ≈ 11 to suppress $H^+$ interference. Also contains $NH_4^+$ to suppress $K^+$ interference.
- **Measurement Cell:** A continuous flow-through cell with temperature-controlled housing and automatic calibration (auto-cleaning options available for power-plant use).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Topic | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|-------|---------------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Nernst (general) | $E = E^0 - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log \dfrac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}$ | $E$ = cell potential (V); $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $T$ = absolute temp (K); $n$ = e⁻ transferred; $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol | At $25\,°C$, $\frac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 0.05916$ V |
| 2 | pH Meter | $E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - 0.05916 \cdot pH$ | $E_{cell}$ measured (V); $pH$ dimensionless | Slope = 0.05916 V/pH at 298.15 K |
| 3 | Conductivity | $\kappa = K_{cell} \cdot G = \dfrac{l}{A} \cdot G$ | $\kappa$ in S/cm; $K_{cell}$ in cm⁻¹; $G$ in S | $K_{cell}$ typically 0.1, 1.0, or 10 cm⁻¹ |
| 4 | Molar Conductivity | $\Lambda_m = \dfrac{\kappa}{c}$ | $\Lambda_m$ in S·cm²/mol; $c$ in mol/cm³ | Used in analytical chemistry to compare electrolytes |
| 5 | Temperature Comp. (Cond.) | $\kappa_{25} = \dfrac{\kappa_T}{1 + \alpha(T - 25)}$ | $\alpha \approx 0.02 /°C$ | Linear model; ISO 7888 gives non-linear curve |
| 6 | Beer–Lambert (IR Analyser) | $A = \varepsilon \, c \, l = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | $A$ = absorbance; $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity; $l$ = path length (cm) | Valid for dilute, non-scattering media |
| 7 | Zirconia (Nernst) | $E = \dfrac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{O_2,\,ref}}{p_{O_2,\,sample}}\right)$ | $p_{O_2}$ partial pressure of $O_2$ | Operating T > 600 °C |
| 8 | DO Clark Current | $I_d = nFA\,D\, \dfrac{C_0}{\delta}$ | $I_d$ diffusion-limited current; $D$ = diffusivity; $\delta$ = membrane thickness | Linear in $C_0$ (bulk $O_2$ concentration) |
| 9 | Sodium ISE | $E = E^0 + 0.05916 \cdot \log\, a_{Na^+}$ | $a_{Na^+}$ sodium activity | With ISA buffer (pH ≈ 11) |
| 10 | Paramagnetic Susceptibility | $\chi_{O_2} = 1.44 \times 10^{-2}\,(273/T)^2$ | $\chi$ in cgs | Curie-type temperature dependence |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation for the pH Electrode

**Goal:** Show the exact chain of thermodynamic reasoning leading to the KTU board equation $E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - 0.05916 \cdot pH$.

**Step 1 — Start from the general Nernst equation for a redox half-cell:**

For a single-electrode reaction, the electrode potential $E$ is related to the activities of oxidized ($a_{Ox}$) and reduced ($a_{Red}$) species by

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite the natural logarithm in base 10** using the identity $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute for the glass-membrane half-cell.** The glass electrode response is treated as if the half-reaction were $H^+ + e^- \rightleftharpoons \tfrac{1}{2} H_2$, giving $n = 1$ and an activity ratio of $\dfrac{1}{a_{H^+}}$. Thus,

$$E_{glass} = E^0_{glass} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{a_{H^+}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Use the property of logarithms,** $\log(1/x) = -\log(x)$:

$$E_{glass} = E^0_{glass} + \frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \log_{10}(a_{H^+})$$

**Step 5 — Apply the definition of pH,** $pH = -\log_{10}(a_{H^+})$:

$$E_{glass} = E^0_{glass} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \cdot pH$$

**Step 6 — The measured cell voltage is the difference between the glass electrode and the reference electrode** (both at the same temperature):

$$E_{cell} = E_{glass} - E_{ref} = (E^0_{glass} - E_{ref}) - \frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \cdot pH$$

**Step 7 — Define the consolidated cell constant** $E^0_{cell} = E^0_{glass} - E_{ref}$:

$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \cdot pH$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the constant at 25 °C** ($T = 298.15$ K):

$$\frac{2.303\,RT}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96{,}485} = 0.05916 \text{ V}$$

**Step 9 — Write the final KTU board-result form:**

$$\boxed{E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - 0.05916 \cdot pH \quad (\text{at } 25\,°C)}$$

This is the **fundamental working equation of every pH meter**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Beer–Lambert Law for Infrared Gas Analysers

**Goal:** Show how transmitted IR intensity $I$ relates exponentially to gas concentration $c$.

**Step 1 — Define differential absorption.** For a thin gas slice of thickness $dx$, the fractional decrease in intensity is proportional to the intensity itself, the concentration $c$, and the slice thickness:

$$-\frac{dI}{I} = \varepsilon \, c \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate** from $I = I_0$ at $x = 0$ to $I$ at $x = l$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\varepsilon c \int_0^{l} dx$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\varepsilon c l$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon c l}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to base-10** using $e^{-x} = 10^{-x/2.303}$:

$$I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-(\varepsilon c l/2.303)}$$

**Step 5 — Define absorbance** $A \equiv \log_{10}(I_0/I) = (\varepsilon c l/2.303)$:

$$\boxed{A = \varepsilon \, c \, l = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)}$$

This shows absorbance is *linear* in concentration, which is the foundation for quantitative IR gas analysis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Zirconia Sensor EMF

**Step 1 — Half-cell oxygen reaction** at the zirconia surface:

$$O_2 + 4e^- \rightleftharpoons 2O^{2-}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Nernst** with $n = 4$ electrons per $O_2$:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{2.303\,RT}{4F} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{p_{O_2}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — The standard potential is defined with reference gas** at $p_{O_2,ref}$. Difference between two half-cells exposed to $p_{O_2,ref}$ and $p_{O_2,sample}$:

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2,ref}}{p_{O_2,sample}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Use $R = 8.314$, $F = 96{,}485$, at $T = 1000$ K** (typical sensor temperature):

$$\frac{RT}{4F} = \frac{8.314 \times 1000}{4 \times 96{,}485} = 0.02154 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E = 0.04956 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2,ref}}{p_{O_2,sample}}\right) \text{ V (at 1000 K)}}$$

For an automotive lambda sensor, $p_{O_2,ref} = 0.2095$ atm (air); sample is exhaust gas.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — pH Meter

**Problem (KTU board-type):** A pH combination electrode gives $E_{cell} = +0.218$ V in pH 4.00 buffer at 25 °C. In an unknown solution, the same electrode reads $+0.041$ V. Calculate the unknown pH. Assume Nernstian slope.

**Step 1 — Use the Nernst equation at both readings:**

$$0.218 = E^0_{cell} - 0.05916 \times 4.00$$
$$0.041 = E^0_{cell} - 0.05916 \times pH_x$$

**Step 2 — Subtract to eliminate $E^0_{cell}$:**

$$0.218 - 0.041 = 0.05916 \times (pH_x - 4.00)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $pH_x$:**

$$pH_x = 4.00 + \frac{0.177}{0.05916} = 4.00 + 2.993 = 6.99 \approx 7.00$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The unknown solution is essentially **neutral** (pure water at 25 °C).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Conductivity Meter

**Problem (KTU board-type):** A conductivity cell with $K_{cell} = 1.05$ cm⁻¹ measures a resistance of $4{,}500 \;\Omega$ in a 0.01 M KCl solution at 25 °C. Calculate the conductivity and the molar conductivity of the solution.

**Step 1 — Conductance** $G = 1/R$:

$$G = \frac{1}{4{,}500} = 2.222 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S}$$

**Step 2 — Conductivity** $\kappa = K_{cell} \cdot G$:

$$\kappa = 1.05 \times 2.222 \times 10^{-4} = 2.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S/cm}$$

**Step 3 — Molar concentration** $c = 0.01$ mol/L $= 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ mol/cm³:

$$\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa}{c} = \frac{2.333 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 23.33 \text{ S·cm}^2/\text{mol}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** $\kappa = 2.33 \times 10^{-4}$ S/cm matches the literature value of 1.413 mS/m (scaled), and $\Lambda_m$ lies in the expected range for dilute KCl at 25 °C.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Zirconia Oxygen Sensor

**Problem (KTU board-type):** A zirconia sensor operates at 800 °C with air reference ($p_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm). The measured EMF is $E = +0.035$ V. Calculate $p_{O_2,sample}$.

**Step 1 — Use the Nernst form** with $T = 1073$ K:

$$\frac{RT}{4F} = \frac{8.314 \times 1073}{4 \times 96{,}485} = 0.02312 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$0.035 = 0.02312 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{0.21}{p_{O_2,sample}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the log ratio:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.21}{p_{O_2,sample}}\right) = \frac{0.035}{0.02312} = 1.514$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate:**

$$\frac{0.21}{p_{O_2,sample}} = e^{1.514} = 4.545$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**

$$p_{O_2,sample} = \frac{0.21}{4.545} = 0.0462 \text{ atm} = 4.62\% \text{ O}_2$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** This corresponds to a **lean-burn exhaust gas**, typical of an efficient internal-combustion engine.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: pH Meter Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Unknown Solution pH_x] --> B[Glass Membrane Electrode]
    B --> C[Internal Buffer pH 7.0]
    C --> D[Inner Ag/AgCl Reference]
    E[Reference Electrode: Saturated KCl] --> F[Liquid Junction Porous Frit]
    F --> A
    D --> G[High-Impedance Amplifier 10^12 ohm]
    F --> G
    G --> H[ADC and Microprocessor]
    H --> I[Temperature Sensor Pt1000]
    I --> H
    H --> J[LCD Display pH Reading]
    H --> K[4-20 mA Analog Output]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Oscillator 1 kHz Sine] --> B[Conductivity Cell Two or Four Electrodes]
    B --> C[Solution Under Test]
    C --> B
    B --> D[I-to-V Converter Transimpedance]
    D --> E[Rectifier and RMS Detector]
    E --> F[Microcontroller]
    G[Temperature Sensor Pt100] --> F
    F --> H[Linearization and Temp Compensation]
    H --> I[Display in S/cm or µS/cm]
    F --> J[Auto-ranging Cell Constant 0.1 to 10 cm^-1]
```

```mermaid
    D --> G[Current-to-Voltage Converter]
    E --> G

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Q:** What is the principle of a paramagnetic oxygen analyser? Why is it selective to oxygen?

**Model Answer:**
A paramagnetic oxygen analyser is based on the fact that oxygen is the only common industrial gas that is **strongly paramagnetic** (magnetic susceptibility $\chi_{O_2} \approx +3449 \times 10^{-6}$ cgs), while all other common gases ($N_2$, $CO_2$, $CH_4$, $H_2$) are weakly **diamagnetic** ($\chi \approx -0.4$ to $-1 \times 10^{-6}$ cgs). When the gas sample passes through a non-uniform magnetic field, oxygen molecules are drawn toward the strongest field, displacing a nitrogen-filled test body suspended on a torsion filament. The displacement is sensed by an optical lever and photocell, and the restoring current in a feedback coil is proportional to the **partial pressure of oxygen ($p_{O_2}$)**. Because no other major gas responds, the instrument is highly selective to oxygen and is therefore widely used in flue-gas analysis and combustion control. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Q:** Differentiate between a polarographic (Clark) and a galvanic dissolved oxygen probe.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Polarographic (Clark) DO Probe | Galvanic DO Probe |
|---|---|---|
| Polarization voltage | Requires external voltage of **0.6–0.8 V** | Self-polarizing, no external voltage |
| Electrode materials | Pt cathode, Ag/AgCl anode | Pb or Zn anode, Ag cathode |
| Time to stabilise | Long (1–2 h for new membrane) | Short (5–15 min) |
| Flow sensitivity | More flow-sensitive | Less flow-sensitive |
| Electrolyte | KCl buffer | Alkaline electrolyte (e.g., KOH) |
| Typical application | Lab and field BOD measurement | Field spot-check, in-situ process |

The polarographic probe needs a *polarising source*, whereas the galvanic probe works like a primary cell, the anode metal being consumed during operation. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A **[14 Marks, Internal Choice Option 1]**
**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **zirconia-oxide oxygen analyser**. Derive the Nernst equation used in its operation.

**(b) [7 marks]** A zirconia sensor at 950 °C produces an EMF of 100 mV with air as the reference. Calculate the partial pressure of oxygen in the sample gas.

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Construction:** Calcia-stabilized zirconia (CSZ) ceramic tube with porous Pt electrodes on inner and outer surfaces, housed in a furnace maintaining 600–1100 °C.
- **Working:** Reference gas (air) on one side, sample gas on the other; $O_2$ ionises at the higher-$p_{O_2}$ side, migrates through the electrolyte, and re-combines on the lower side, generating an EMF.
- **Derivation:** Starting from the Nernst equation applied to $O_2 + 4e^- \rightleftharpoons 2O^{2-}$ with $n = 4$:

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2,\,ref}}{p_{O_2,\,sample}}\right)$$

- **Explanation:** At 25 °C the constant $\frac{RT}{4F} = 0.0142$ V (using $\ln$), or 0.0065 V (using $\log_{10}$). The output is linear in $\ln(p_{O_2,ref}/p_{O_2,sample})$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $T = 950 + 273 = 1223$ K, $E = 0.100$ V, $p_{O_2,ref} = 0.2095$ atm.

**Step 1 — Compute slope:**
$$\frac{RT}{4F} = \frac{8.314 \times 1223}{4 \times 96{,}485} = 0.02635 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$0.100 = 0.02635 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{0.2095}{p_{O_2,sm}}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.2095}{p_{O_2,sm}}\right) = \frac{0.100}{0.02635} = 3.795$$

$$\frac{0.2095}{p_{O_2,sm}} = e^{3.795} = 44.50$$

$$p_{O_2,sm} = \frac{0.2095}{44.50} = 4.71 \times 10^{-3} \text{ atm} = 0.471\%$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** The sample gas contains approximately **0.471 % oxygen**, typical of an oxygen-depleted combustion exhaust. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B **[14 Marks, Internal Choice Option 2]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the construction and working of a **glass-electrode pH meter** with the help of a diagram. State the Nernst equation and explain how the slope varies with temperature.

**(b) [7 marks]** A glass pH electrode gives $E_{cell} = +0.412$ V in a pH 6.86 buffer at 25 °C. When dipped in an unknown solution it reads $+0.318$ V. Determine the pH of the unknown.

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Construction:** A thin-walled bulb of special Na-ion-sensitive glass fused to a stem, filled with internal buffer (pH 7.0) and an internal Ag/AgCl reference wire. The reference electrode (SCE or Ag/AgCl with KCl) is housed in the same probe (combination electrode) and contacts the sample via a porous liquid junction.
- **Working:** $H^+$ ions from the sample exchange with $Na^+$ in the outer gel layer of the glass, generating a **phase-boundary potential** that follows the Nernst equation. The difference between this potential and the constant reference potential is converted by a high-impedance amplifier into a stable pH reading.
- **Nernst Equation:**
$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - 0.05916 \cdot pH \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$
- **Temperature Dependence:** The slope $\frac{2.303\,RT}{F}$ is *directly proportional* to absolute temperature — at 50 °C it is 0.0641 V/pH, at 5 °C it is 0.0552 V/pH. Hence automatic temperature compensation is mandatory for accurate pH measurement. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (b):**
**Given:** pH 6.86 gives $E = 0.412$ V; unknown gives $E = 0.318$ V; T = 25 °C → slope = 0.05916 V/pH.

**Step 1 — Eliminate $E^0_{cell}$ by subtraction:**
$$E_1 - E_2 = 0.05916 \cdot (pH_2 - pH_1)$$
$$0.412 - 0.318 = 0.05916 \cdot (pH_x - 6.86)$$

**Step 2 — Solve:**
$$pH_x - 6.86 = \frac{0.094}{0.05916} = 1.589$$
$$pH_x = 6.86 + 1.589 = 8.45$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** The unknown solution is **mildly basic** with pH ≈ 8.45. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units & Constants Trap:** Many students write $E = 0.059 \cdot pH$ without specifying "at 25 °C," and lose 1 mark. The Nernst slope is *temperature-dependent*; always quote both.
> 2. **Sign Convention:** pH is *subtracted* from $E^0_{cell}$ — getting the sign wrong gives an inverted (acidic-looking basic solution) answer that costs full marks in derivation questions.
> 3. **IR Analyser Chopper:** Students often forget to mention the **chopper wheel** and **lock-in detection at chop frequency** — these are mandatory for 2 marks in a 7-mark question.
> 4. **DO Membrane Material:** The membrane is **Teflon (FEP)**, not any polymer — examiners explicitly test this.
> 5. **Sodium Analyser Buffer:** The ISA buffer contains an *amine* to raise pH ≈ 11, *not* an acid; this is a frequent error.
> 6. **Conductivity Cell Constant Units:** Students confuse $K_{cell}$ (cm⁻¹) with conductivity (S/cm); note they are *different* quantities and have *different* units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Industrial Gas Analysers** are continuous, on-line instruments; key types are Thermal Conductivity, **Paramagnetic (O₂)**, **Infrared**, and **Zirconia**.
- **Thermal Conductivity** works on heat-loss difference; ideal for binary mixtures like $H_2/N_2$.
- **Paramagnetic Oxygen** is the gold standard for $O_2$ because $O_2$ is the only strongly paramagnetic common gas; magnetic susceptibility obeys the **Curie-type** law.
- **Infrared Analyser** uses molecular vibration–rotation absorption obeying the **Beer–Lambert law**; suitable for polyatomic gases.
- **Zirconia Analyser** is a high-temperature (T > 600 °C) oxygen-ion conductor with EMF = $\frac{RT}{4F} \ln(p_{O_2,ref}/p_{O_2,sample})$.
- **Chemiluminescence** is the standard for $NO_x$ measurement via $NO + O_3$ reaction.
- **pH Meter** = high-impedance voltmeter + glass electrode; $E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - 0.05916 \cdot pH$ at 25 °C.
- Always **calibrate** with at least two buffers (typically pH 4, 7, 10) and use a **temperature probe** for compensation.
- **Conductivity** is the reciprocal of resistivity; cell constant $K_{cell} = l/A$ in cm⁻¹.
- AC excitation (1 kHz typical) avoids **polarization** at low conductivities.
- **DO (Clark) Probe** uses an **$O_2$-permeable Teflon membrane** and a polarizing voltage of 0.6–0.8 V; current $\propto C_{O_2}$.
- DO units are **mg/L** (or % saturation); at 25 °C in air-saturated water, DO = 8.26 mg/L.
- **Sodium Analyser** uses **lithia-alumina-silica glass** selective to $Na^+$; works in ppb range for boiler feedwater.
- ISA buffer (pH ≈ 11, with diisopropylamine) is essential to suppress $H^+$ interference.
- Always use **double-junction reference** with $K^+$-free filling to prevent cross-contamination.
- All Nernstian electrodes need **high-impedance ($>10^{12}\;\Omega$)** measuring circuits to prevent current draw and potential drift.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Study Tip:** Memorize the Nernst equation at 25 °C ($\frac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 0.05916$ V), the Beer–Lambert equation ($A = \varepsilon c l$), and the zirconia EMF equation — these three alone can secure ≥ 60 % of marks in any Part-B question on this module. Always draw a labelled block diagram: examiners award 2–3 marks purely for the diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chromatographic Process: Classification, Terms in Chromatography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT521 - ANALYTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chromatographic Process: Classification, Terms in Chromatography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chromatography** is a physical method of separation, identification, and quantitative estimation of the components of a mixture, in which the components to be separated are distributed between two phases: a **stationary phase** (which remains fixed in space) and a **mobile phase** (which percolates through or along the stationary phase in a definite direction).

The term **Chromatography** was first coined by the Russian botanist **Mikhail Tswett in 1906** to describe his experiments on the separation of plant leaf pigments (chlorophylls, xanthophylls, carotenes) using a calcium carbonate packed glass column. The components moved as colored bands, hence the name *chroma* (color) + *graphein* (to write).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Filter Coffee Maker ☕**
> Imagine making South Indian filter coffee. Ground coffee powder is mixed with water and poured into a brass filter. The coffee bed acts as the **stationary phase**, and the water draining down is the **mobile phase**. The water selectively extracts the flavor compounds (caffeine, oils) at different rates — the heavy oils stick to the powder (high retention), while the bitter compounds elute first. Each drop leaving the filter is the **eluate**, and the volume that comes out before any coffee flavor appears is the **dead volume** (just plain water that simply filled the empty pores of the bed).
> Chromatography works on exactly the same principle — different components interact differently with the two phases and therefore travel at different speeds, becoming separated.

### 1.3 Why Chromatography is Used in Analytical Instrumentation

Chromatography is the **workhorse separation technique** of modern analytical laboratories because:
- It can separate **complex multi-component mixtures** (petroleum, pharmaceuticals, biological fluids, environmental samples).
- It provides both **qualitative** (identification via retention time) and **quantitative** (peak area/height) information.
- When coupled with detectors (FID, TCD, MS, UV-Vis), it forms the powerful class of instruments known as **hyphenated techniques** (e.g., GC-MS, LC-MS).
- Sensitivity down to **parts-per-billion (ppb)** levels is routinely achievable.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Note:** Always remember — *every chromatographic separation is governed by differences in the equilibrium distribution of analytes between the stationary and mobile phases.* If the distribution constants are equal, no separation occurs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Principle of the Chromatographic Process

The chromatographic process follows a logical operational sequence:

1. **Sample Introduction (Injection):** A small, precise volume of the analyte mixture is introduced at the inlet of the chromatographic system.
2. **Mobile Phase Transport:** The mobile phase (gas or liquid) carries the sample forward through the stationary phase under a pressure gradient or capillary action.
3. **Differential Partitioning:** Each component partitions between the mobile phase and the stationary phase. Components with stronger affinity for the stationary phase lag behind (higher retention); components preferring the mobile phase elute earlier.
4. **Band Broadening:** Due to diffusion, eddy diffusion, and mass transfer effects, each component spreads into a Gaussian-shaped band as it travels.
5. **Detection:** At the outlet, a detector converts the eluted concentration into an electrical signal.
6. **Recording:** The signal vs. time plot is the **chromatogram**, showing peaks corresponding to each separated component.

### 2.2 Classification of Chromatographic Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Chromatography is classified along **four orthogonal axes** as per KTU syllabus. Examiners frequently ask for this classification in both 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

#### A. Based on Shape of the Chromatographic Bed

| Bed Shape | Sub-Technique | Stationary Phase Format |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Planar** | Paper Chromatography (PC) | Paper sheet (cellulose) |
| **Planar** | Thin-Layer Chromatography (TLC) | Thin layer of silica/alumina on glass plate |
| **Column** | Packed Column Chromatography | Solid particles in a tube |
| **Column** | Open Tubular (Capillary) | Liquid film on inner wall of narrow tube |

#### B. Based on Physical State of the Mobile Phase

| Mobile Phase | Technique Name | Common Abbreviation |
|--------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Gas** | Gas Chromatography | GC |
| **Liquid** | Liquid Chromatography | LC |
| **Supercritical Fluid** | Supercritical Fluid Chromatography | SFC |

#### C. Based on the Mechanism / Principle of Separation

| Mechanism | Principle |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Adsorption Chromatography** | Differential adsorption on solid surface (e.g., silica gel) |
| **Partition Chromatography** | Differential solubility in two immiscible liquids |
| **Ion-Exchange Chromatography** | Differential exchange of ions with charged resin |
| **Size-Exclusion (Gel) Chromatography** | Differential penetration into porous matrix |
| **Affinity Chromatography** | Specific biochemical lock-and-key interaction |
| **Chiral Chromatography** | Differential interaction with chiral stationary phase |

#### D. Based on the Mode of Development

| Mode | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Elution Development** | Mobile phase passed continuously; components elute as separate peaks (most common) |
| **Frontal Development** | Sample continuously fed; least-retained component elutes first in pure form |
| **Displacement Development** | Strongly retained displacer pushes sample components ahead as adjacent bands |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 12 equations — they cover 90 % of numerical questions in KTU ESE for Module 3.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Retention Factor** (k') | $k' = \dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \dfrac{V_R - V_M}{V_M}$ | dimensionless | How strongly a solute is retained |
| 2 | **Number of Theoretical Plates** | $N = 16 \left(\dfrac{t_R}{W}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Column efficiency |
| 3 | **Alternative N formula** | $N = 5.54 \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Used when half-height width is measured |
| 4 | **Plate Height** | $H = \dfrac{L}{N}$ | mm or cm | Efficiency per unit length |
| 5 | **Resolution** | $R_s = \dfrac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{W_1 + W_2}$ | dimensionless | Quality of separation |
| 6 | **Selectivity Factor** | $\alpha = \dfrac{k'_2}{k'_1}$ | dimensionless | Thermodynamic selectivity |
| 7 | **Relative Retention (α)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{t'_{R2}}{t'_{R1}}$ | dimensionless | Same as selectivity |
| 8 | **Linear Mobile Phase Velocity** | $u = \dfrac{L}{t_M}$ | cm/s | Mobile phase linear speed |
| 9 | **Van Deemter Equation** | $H = A + \dfrac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$ | mm | Optimum plate height |
| 10 | **Distribution Constant** | $K = \dfrac{C_s}{C_m}$ | dimensionless | Partition equilibrium |
| 11 | **Peak Capacity** | $n = 1 + \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \ln \dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}$ | dimensionless | Max resolvable peaks |
| 12 | **Trennzahl Number** | $TZ = \dfrac{t_{R(n+1)} - t_{R(n)}}{w_{1/2(n)} + w_{1/2(n+1)}} - 1$ | dimensionless | Practical peak capacity |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Purity analysis of drugs, impurity profiling (mandated by ICH Q3A/B guidelines).
- **Petrochemical Industry:** Hydrocarbon analysis in crude oil using GC with FID.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Detection of pesticides, PCBs, dioxins at ppb levels.
- **Forensic Science:** Identification of drugs, explosives, and toxic substances.
- **Food Industry:** Quality control of flavors, fatty acid profiles, pesticide residues.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Retention Factor (k')

Consider a solute molecule traversing a chromatographic column. The fraction of time it spends in the mobile phase is $t_M / t_R$, and in the stationary phase is $t'_R / t_R$.

$$
\frac{t_M}{t_R} = \frac{\text{amount in mobile phase}}{\text{total amount}}
$$

The **retention factor** is defined as the ratio of the time spent in the stationary phase to the time spent in the mobile phase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k' &= \frac{t'_R}{t_M} \\
&= \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

- **Step 1:** Identify the dead time $t_M$ — the time the mobile phase itself takes to traverse the column (unretained component).
- **Step 2:** Identify the total retention time $t_R$ — the time the solute takes to traverse the column.
- **Step 3:** The *additional* time the solute spends due to interaction with the stationary phase is $t_R - t_M = t'_R$ (the **adjusted retention time**).
- **Step 4:** Normalize this extra time by $t_M$ to obtain a dimensionless retention factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Number of Theoretical Plates (N)

The Plate Theory (Martin & Synge, 1941) treats the chromatographic column as a series of discrete, narrow "theoretical plates," similar to a distillation column. Each plate allows one equilibrium partitioning step.

For a Gaussian peak eluted from $N$ plates, the relationship between the peak width at baseline ($W$) and the retention time is empirical. Consider the **standard deviation σ** of a Gaussian distribution:

$$
\sigma = \frac{W}{4}
$$

This comes from the fact that for a Gaussian peak, the baseline width $W$ spans $\pm 2\sigma$ on either side of the peak center (total = $4\sigma$).

The standard deviation σ in time units relates to retention time and column length as:

$$
\sigma = \frac{t_R}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

Substituting $\sigma = W/4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{W}{4} &= \frac{t_R}{\sqrt{N}} \\
\sqrt{N} &= \frac{4 t_R}{W} \\
N &= \frac{16 t_R^2}{W^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{N = 16\left(\frac{t_R}{W}\right)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternate derivation using half-height width $w_{1/2}$:**

For a Gaussian, the half-height width is $w_{1/2} = 2.354\,\sigma$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{w_{1/2}}{2.354} \\
\frac{w_{1/2}}{2.354} &= \frac{t_R}{\sqrt{N}} \\
\sqrt{N} &= \frac{2.354\, t_R}{w_{1/2}} \\
N &= \frac{(2.354)^2 t_R^2}{w_{1/2}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{N = 5.54\left(\frac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Resolution Equation (Purnell Equation)

Resolution is the **master equation** of chromatography. The Purnell equation relates resolution to efficiency, selectivity, and retention:

$$
R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{(\alpha - 1)}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}
$$

**Derivation Walkthrough:**

- **Step 1 — Peak separation in time:** Two adjacent peaks elute at $t_{R1}$ and $t_{R2}$ with baseline widths $W_1$ and $W_2$.

$$
\Delta t_R = t_{R2} - t_{R1}
$$

- **Step 2 — Average peak width:**

$$
\bar{W} = \frac{W_1 + W_2}{2}
$$

- **Step 3 — Definition of resolution:**

$$
R_s = \frac{\Delta t_R}{\bar{W}} = \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{W_1 + W_2}
$$

- **Step 4 — Express $t_R$ in terms of $t_M$ and $k'$:** Using $t_R = t_M(1 + k')$:

$$
t_{R2} - t_{R1} = t_M(k'_2 - k'_1)
$$

- **Step 5 — Express $W$ in terms of $N$ and $t_R$:** Using $W = 4 t_R / \sqrt{N}$:

$$
W_i = \frac{4 t_R^{(i)}}{\sqrt{N}} = \frac{4 t_M(1 + k'_i)}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

- **Step 6 — Substitute into resolution expression:**

$$
R_s = \frac{t_M(k'_2 - k'_1)}{4 t_M(1 + k'_2)/\sqrt{N}} = \frac{\sqrt{N}(k'_2 - k'_1)}{4(1 + k'_2)}
$$

- **Step 7 — Factor out $k'_1$ and introduce $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1$:**

$$
R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}\, k'_1 (\alpha - 1)}{4(1 + k'_2)} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{\alpha(1 + k'_2)} \cdot (\alpha - 1)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation of the Three Terms:**

| Term | Physical Meaning | How to Improve |
|------|------------------|----------------|
| $\sqrt{N} / 4$ | **Efficiency term** — column length, plate count | Increase column length, reduce particle size |
| $(\alpha - 1)/\alpha$ | **Selectivity term** — chemistry of separation | Change stationary/mobile phase chemistry, temperature |
| $k'_2 / (1 + k'_2)$ | **Retention term** — retention strength | Optimize $k'$ to range **2 – 10** |

### 3.4 Derivation of the Van Deemter Equation

The Van Deemter equation describes how plate height $H$ varies with mobile phase linear velocity $u$. Three independent broadening mechanisms contribute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= H_{\text{eddy}} + H_{\text{longitudinal}} + H_{\text{mass transfer}} \\
&= A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u
\end{aligned}
$$

| Term | Physical Origin | Effect |
|------|-----------------|--------|
| $A = 2\lambda d_p$ | **Eddy Diffusion** — multiple flow paths around packing particles | Independent of $u$ |
| $B = 2\gamma D_m$ | **Longitudinal Molecular Diffusion** — Brownian spreading in mobile phase | Dominates at low $u$ |
| $C = \frac{8}{\pi^2}\cdot\frac{k'}{(1+k')^2}\cdot\frac{d_f^2}{D_s}$ | **Mass Transfer Resistance** — slow equilibration in stationary film | Dominates at high $u$ |

To find the **optimum velocity** $u_{\text{opt}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dH}{du} &= -\frac{B}{u^2} + C = 0 \\
u_{\text{opt}} &= \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{min}} &= A + 2\sqrt{BC}
\end{aligned}
$$

This explains the characteristic **U-shape** of the Van Deemter curve: at very low $u$, longitudinal diffusion dominates (high $H$); at very high $u$, mass transfer resistance dominates (high $H$); an optimum exists in between.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A solute elutes with retention time $t_R = 9.0$ min. The dead time is $t_M = 1.0$ min. The baseline peak width is $W = 1.0$ min. Calculate (a) the retention factor $k'$, (b) the number of theoretical plates $N$, and (c) the plate height if the column length is $L = 25$ cm.

**Solution:**

**(a) Retention factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k' &= \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} \\
&= \frac{9.0 - 1.0}{1.0} \\
&= 8.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Number of theoretical plates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 16\left(\frac{t_R}{W}\right)^2 \\
&= 16\left(\frac{9.0}{1.0}\right)^2 \\
&= 16 \times 81 \\
&= 1296 \text{ plates}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Plate height:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{L}{N} \\
&= \frac{25\,\text{cm}}{1296} \\
&= 0.0193\,\text{cm} \\
&= 0.193\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Answer Writing Tip:** Always state the units explicitly. The examiner looks for "$H = 0.193$ mm" rather than just "0.0193 cm."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Chromatographic Process Flow

This block diagram maps the functional architecture of any chromatographic system, regardless of whether it is GC, LC, or TLC.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sample Injection Port] --> B[Mobile Phase Reservoir or Carrier Gas Supply]
    B --> C[Injector Block where Sample Mixes with Mobile Phase]
    C --> D[Chromatographic Column Containing Stationary Phase]
    D --> E[Detector Module FID TCD UV MS etc]
    E --> F[Signal Amplifier and Integrator]
    F --> G[Recorder or Computer Data Station]
    G --> H[Chromatogram Plot Signal vs Time]
```

### 4.2 Classification of Chromatographic Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Chromatography]
    A --> B[Based on Bed Shape]
    A --> C[Based on Mobile Phase State]
    A --> D[Based on Separation Mechanism]
    A --> E[Based on Development Mode]

    B --> B1[Planar]
    B --> B2[Column]
    B1 --> B1a[Paper Chromatography]
    B1 --> B1b[Thin Layer Chromatography TLC]
    B2 --> B2a[Packed Column]
    B2 --> B2b[Capillary Column]

    C --> C1[Gas Chromatography GC]
    C --> C2[Liquid Chromatography LC]
    C --> C3[Supercritical Fluid Chromatography SFC]

    D --> D1[Adsorption]
    D --> D2[Partition]
    D --> D3[Ion Exchange]
    D --> D4[Size Exclusion]
    D --> D5[Affinity]

    E --> E1[Elution Mode]
    E --> E2[Frontal Mode]
    E --> E3[Displacement Mode]
```

### 4.3 Key Retention Parameters on a Chromatogram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chromatogram Time Axis] --> B[Injection Point t = 0]
    B --> C[tM Dead Time Solvent Peak]
    C --> D[tR1 Retention Time Peak 1]
    C --> E[tR2 Retention Time Peak 2]
    D --> F[W1 Baseline Width Peak 1]
    E --> G[W2 Baseline Width Peak 2]
    D --> H[tR prime 1 Adjusted Retention]
    E --> I[tR prime 2 Adjusted Retention]
```

### 4.4 Effect of k-prime on Peak Position

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[k prime value] --> B[k prime less than 1]
    A --> C[k prime between 2 and 10]
    A --> D[k prime greater than 20]

    B --> E[Peaks elute too fast Poor Resolution]
    C --> F[Optimum Resolution and Analysis Time]
    D --> G[Excessively Long Retention Broad Peaks]
```

### 4.5 Van Deemter Plot — Optimum Velocity Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Van Deemter Plate Height vs Velocity] --> B[Region 1 Low u]
    A --> C[Region 2 Optimum u opt]
    A --> D[Region 3 High u]

    B --> B1[Longitudinal Diffusion Dominates B over u Large H]
    C --> C1[Eddy Diffusion Balanced by Mass Transfer Minimum H]
    D --> D1[Mass Transfer Resistance Dominates C times u Large H]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to draw the Van Deemter plot, label the three regions clearly and mark $u_{\text{opt}}$ with a vertical dashed line and $H_{\text{min}}$ on the y-axis. Mentioning $H_{\text{min}} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$ carries bonus credit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define chromatography. Mention the basic principle on which it operates.**

**Model Answer:**

Chromatography, as per IUPAC, is a physical method of separation in which the components of a mixture are distributed between two phases — a stationary phase (which remains fixed) and a mobile phase (which moves in a definite direction). The basic principle is **differential partitioning** of mixture components between the two phases. Each component exhibits a characteristic **distribution constant** $K = C_s / C_m$, where $C_s$ and $C_m$ are the equilibrium concentrations in the stationary and mobile phases respectively. Components with higher $K$ values are retained longer by the stationary phase, while those with lower $K$ values elute earlier with the mobile phase. This difference in equilibrium distribution produces the separation. *(3 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between retention time and dead time with the help of a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Dead Time ($t_M$):** The time required for the mobile phase (or an unretained solute) to travel from the injection point to the detector. It represents the time the mobile phase itself spends in the column.
- **Retention Time ($t_R$):** The total time a retained solute takes to travel from the injection point to the peak maximum at the detector.

The relationship is:
$$
t_R = t_M + t'_R
$$
where $t'_R$ is the **adjusted retention time**, representing the *additional* time the solute is held back by the stationary phase.

In the chromatogram, $t_M$ is marked by the solvent front (first perturbation or air peak), while $t_R$ is measured at the apex of the analyte peak. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

##### **(a) Classify the chromatographic techniques in detail with suitable examples.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Chromatographic techniques are classified along **four major axes**:

**1. Based on the Shape of the Chromatographic Bed (Planar vs Column):**

| Type | Sub-Techniques | Description |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| **Planar** | Paper Chromatography (PC), Thin-Layer Chromatography (TLC) | Separation occurs on a flat sheet/plate |
| **Column** | Packed Column, Open Tubular (Capillary) | Separation occurs inside a tube |

**2. Based on the Physical State of the Mobile Phase:**

| Mobile Phase | Technique | Typical Application |
|--------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Gas** | Gas Chromatography (GC) | Volatile organics, hydrocarbons |
| **Liquid** | Liquid Chromatography (HPLC, LC) | Pharmaceuticals, biomolecules |
| **Supercritical Fluid** | Supercritical Fluid Chromatography (SFC) | Non-volatile, thermally labile compounds |

**3. Based on the Mechanism of Separation:**

| Mechanism | Principle | Example |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **Adsorption** | Differential surface adsorption on solid | Silica gel chromatography |
| **Partition** | Differential solubility in two immiscible liquids | Reversed-phase HPLC |
| **Ion-Exchange** | Exchange of analyte ions with resin ions | Water softening, amino acid analysis |
| **Size-Exclusion** | Differential pore penetration | Gel permeation chromatography of polymers |
| **Affinity** | Specific lock-and-key interaction | Antibody purification |

**4. Based on the Mode of Development:**

- **Elution Mode:** Mobile phase continuously passed; components elute as peaks.
- **Frontal Mode:** Sample continuously introduced; only the least-retained component elutes pure initially.
- **Displacement Mode:** A strong displacer pushes sample components as adjacent bands. *(7 marks — with diagrams or tables carrying weight)*

---

##### **(b) Define the following terms: Retention factor, Selectivity factor, Plate height, and Resolution. Explain their significance.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Retention Factor ($k'$):**
$$
k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}
$$
It is a dimensionless measure of how strongly a solute is retained by the stationary phase relative to the mobile phase. Ideal $k'$ lies between **2 and 10** for analytical chromatography. Lower values give poor separation; higher values cause long analysis time and broad peaks.

**(ii) Selectivity Factor ($\alpha$):**
$$
\alpha = \frac{k'_2}{k'_1} \quad (\text{with } k'_2 > k'_1)
$$
It measures the relative retention of two adjacent peaks, reflecting the **thermodynamic selectivity** of the column. $\alpha > 1$ is required for separation; higher $\alpha$ allows easier separation.

**(iii) Plate Height ($H$):**
$$
H = \frac{L}{N}
$$
It represents the efficiency per unit length of the column, in mm or cm. Smaller $H$ indicates a more efficient column. $H$ depends on mobile phase velocity through the Van Deemter equation.

**(iv) Resolution ($R_s$):**
$$
R_s = \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{W_1 + W_2}
$$
It is the master quantitative measure of how well two adjacent peaks are separated. **$R_s = 1.0$ indicates ~98 % separation** (touching at baseline); **$R_s \geq 1.5$** is considered baseline separation (required for quantitative analysis). *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

##### **(a) With a neat diagram, explain the Van Deemter equation. Discuss the significance of each term and the optimum velocity.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of Van Deemter Equation:**
$$
H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u
$$

**The Three Contributing Terms:**

**1. Eddy Diffusion Term ($A = 2\lambda d_p$):**
- $\lambda$ = packing irregularity factor
- $d_p$ = average particle diameter of packing
- Caused by multiple flow paths around stationary phase particles. **Independent of mobile phase velocity.**
- Reduced by using smaller, more uniform particles and well-packed columns.

**2. Longitudinal Diffusion Term ($B/u$):**
- $B = 2\gamma D_m$ where $\gamma$ is an obstruction factor and $D_m$ is the solute diffusion coefficient in mobile phase
- Caused by Brownian motion of solute molecules in the mobile phase. **Dominates at low velocities.**
- Higher $D_m$ (gases) makes this term more important in GC than in LC.

**3. Mass Transfer Term ($C \cdot u$):**
$$
C = \frac{8}{\pi^2}\cdot\frac{k'}{(1+k')^2}\cdot\frac{d_f^2}{D_s}
$$
where $d_f$ = stationary phase film thickness, $D_s$ = diffusion coefficient in stationary phase.
- Caused by finite time required for solute to equilibrate between mobile and stationary phases. **Dominates at high velocities.**
- Reduced by using thinner stationary phase films.

**Optimum Velocity:**
Setting $\dfrac{dH}{du} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{B}{u^2} + C &= 0 \\
u_{\text{opt}} &= \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}} \\
H_{\text{min}} &= A + 2\sqrt{BC}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Van Deemter curve has a characteristic U-shape. Operating at $u_{\text{opt}}$ minimizes $H$ and maximizes column efficiency. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **(b) Two compounds A and B elute from a column with retention times 6.5 min and 7.8 min respectively. The peak widths at baseline are 0.40 min and 0.45 min respectively. The dead time is 1.0 min. Calculate: (i) Resolution, (ii) Number of theoretical plates (average), (iii) Selectivity factor, (iv) Retention factor of compound B.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $t_{R,A} = 6.5$ min, $W_A = 0.40$ min
- $t_{R,B} = 7.8$ min, $W_B = 0.45$ min
- $t_M = 1.0$ min

**(i) Resolution:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_s &= \frac{2(t_{R,B} - t_{R,A})}{W_A + W_B} \\
&= \frac{2(7.8 - 6.5)}{0.40 + 0.45} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 1.3}{0.85} \\
&= \frac{2.6}{0.85} \\
&= 3.06
\end{aligned}
$$
Since $R_s > 1.5$, the peaks are baseline-separated. *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Number of Theoretical Plates (using average peak data):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{t_R} &= \frac{6.5 + 7.8}{2} = 7.15\,\text{min} \\
\bar{W} &= \frac{0.40 + 0.45}{2} = 0.425\,\text{min} \\
N &= 16 \left(\frac{\bar{t_R}}{\bar{W}}\right)^2 \\
&= 16 \left(\frac{7.15}{0.425}\right)^2 \\
&= 16 \times (16.8235)^2 \\
&= 16 \times 283.0 \\
&= 4528
\end{aligned}
$$
*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Selectivity Factor:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
k'_A &= \frac{6.5 - 1.0}{1.0} = 5.5 \\
k'_B &= \frac{7.8 - 1.0}{1.0} = 6.8 \\
\alpha &= \frac{k'_B}{k'_A} = \frac{6.8}{5.5} = 1.236
\end{aligned}
$$
*(1.5 marks)*

**(iv) Retention Factor of B:** Already calculated as $k'_B = 6.8$ *(dimensionless)*. *(1.5 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write $k' = t_R / t_M$. The correct formula is $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$. Losing 1 mark here is the single most common error in Module 3 numericals.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using $W$ in the equation $N = 5.54 (t_R / w_{1/2})^2$ — students often mix up the two formulas. $W$ is **baseline width**, $w_{1/2}$ is **half-height width**. The constants differ (16 vs. 5.54).
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Reporting $R_s$ without comment. Always state whether the value is acceptable ($R_s \geq 1.5$ for baseline resolution).
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing "retention factor $k'$" with "retention time $t_R$." They are dimensionally and physically different quantities.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** In classification questions, students often miss the **"Mode of Development"** category (Elution / Frontal / Displacement). Examiners specifically look for this fourth classification axis.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Chromatographic Process**

- ✅ Chromatography = separation by **differential distribution** between a stationary phase and a mobile phase.
- ✅ The term was coined by **Mikhail Tswett (1906)**.
- ✅ Four classification axes: **bed shape, mobile phase state, separation mechanism, development mode**.
- ✅ **Retention time $t_R$** = total time solute spends in column; **Dead time $t_M$** = time of unretained species.
- ✅ **Adjusted retention time** $t'_R = t_R - t_M$.
- ✅ **Retention factor** $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$; ideal range **2 to 10**.
- ✅ **Number of plates** $N = 16 (t_R/W)^2 = 5.54 (t_R/w_{1/2})^2$.
- ✅ **Plate height** $H = L/N$.
- ✅ **Resolution** $R_s = 2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})/(W_1 + W_2)$; baseline separation needs **$R_s \geq 1.5$**.
- ✅ **Selectivity factor** $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1$; must be **$\alpha > 1$** for separation.
- ✅ **Van Deemter equation:** $H = A + B/u + C u$; optimum at $u_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{B/C}$.
- ✅ **Three broadening mechanisms:** Eddy diffusion ($A$, velocity-independent), Longitudinal diffusion ($B/u$, dominant at low $u$), Mass transfer resistance ($C u$, dominant at high $u$).
- ✅ **Master resolution equation (Purnell):** $R_s = (\sqrt{N}/4) \cdot [(\alpha - 1)/\alpha] \cdot [k'_2/(1+k'_2)]$.
- ✅ **HETP** = Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate = $H$.
- ✅ Always label units in numerical answers — examiners deduct marks for missing units.

---
